2. **Step 2 — Wavelength Selection (Excitation Path)**: An excitation filter or monochromator isolates the wavelength that maximally excites the analyte ($\lambda_{ex}$).
3. **Step 3 — Sample Interaction**: The monochromatic beam strikes the sample cell, where fluorophore molecules absorb and re-emit.
4. **Step 4 — Geometric Separation**: Because fluorescence is emitted isotropically in all directions, the detector is placed at **90° to the incident beam** to minimize transmitted excitation light contamination.
5. **Step 5 — Emission Selection**: An emission filter/monochromator isolates the characteristic fluorescence wavelength ($\lambda_{em}$).
6. **Step 6 — Detection**: A photomultiplier tube (PMT) converts photons into a measurable electrical current.
7. **Step 7 — Signal Processing**: Amplification and display of fluorescence intensity.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Quantification of drugs, vitamins, and fluorescent tracers in formulations (detection limits in ppb range).
- **Environmental Monitoring**: Detection of polynuclear aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), oil spills, chlorophyll in water bodies.
- **Bio-medical Diagnostics**: DNA sequencing, immunoassays, malaria detection.
- **Materials Science**: Characterization of OLEDs, fluorescent dyes, and quantum dots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Detailed Working

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Fluorescence Intensity Equation

Starting from first principles of electromagnetic radiation absorption by a uniform absorbing medium:

**Given:**
- Incident radiant power on the sample = $I_0$ (in watts or photons/sec)
- Transmitted radiant power = $I$
- Molar absorptivity = $\varepsilon$ (in L$\cdot$mol$^{-1}\cdot$cm$^{-1}$)
- Optical path length = $b$ (in cm)
- Analyte concentration = $c$ (in mol$\cdot$L$^{-1}$)
- Fluorescence quantum yield = $\phi_f$

**Step 1 — Apply Beer-Lambert Law:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon b c}$$

*Explanation:* The transmitted intensity decreases exponentially with concentration and path length because absorption removes photons from the beam.

**Step 2 — Compute the absorbed radiant power:**

$$I_{abs} = I_0 - I = I_0 - I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon b c} = I_0 \left(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon b c}\right)$$

*Explanation:* The absorbed photons are those that have been removed from the incident beam.

**Step 3 — Multiply by quantum yield to get fluorescence intensity:**

$$I_f = \phi_f \cdot I_{abs} = \phi_f \cdot I_0 \left(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon b c}\right)$$

*Explanation:* Only a fraction $\phi_f$ of absorbed photons is re-emitted as fluorescence; the rest are lost via non-radiative pathways (heat, intersystem crossing, quenching).

**Step 4 — Simplification for dilute solutions:**

For very small values of $x$, the Taylor expansion of $10^{-x}$ around $x=0$ gives:

$$10^{-x} = 1 - x \ln(10) + \frac{(x \ln 10)^2}{2!} - \cdots$$

Since $\ln(10) \approx 2.303$, and for $\varepsilon b c < 0.02$, we truncate after the linear term:

$$10^{-\varepsilon b c} \approx 1 - 2.303 \cdot \varepsilon b c$$

Substituting into F1:

$$I_f = \phi_f \cdot I_0 \left[1 - \left(1 - 2.303 \cdot \varepsilon b c\right)\right]$$

$$\boxed{I_f = 2.303 \cdot \phi_f \cdot I_0 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c}$$

*Conclusion:* At low concentrations, fluorescence intensity is **directly proportional** to analyte concentration. This is the linear dynamic range of fluorimetry.

**Step 5 — Effect of Self-Absorption (Inner Filter Effect):**

At high concentrations ($\varepsilon b c > 0.05$), the emitted fluorescence can be reabsorbed by neighboring fluorophore molecules. This is called the **inner filter effect**, and the linear proportionality breaks down. The fluorescence intensity curve bends downward — hence fluorimeters must be operated within the linear range for quantitative work.

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Fluorimeter Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is KTU University Exam Gold. Students frequently lose marks by confusing filter and monochromator instruments.**

| Parameter | Single Beam Filter Fluorimeter | Double Beam Filter Fluorimeter | Ratio Fluorimeter | Spectrofluorimeter |
|-----------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Wavelength Selector (Excitation)** | Optical Filter | Optical Filter (×2 paths) | Beam splitter + 2 Filters | Grating Monochromator |
| **Wavelength Selector (Emission)** | Optical Filter | Optical Filter | Optical Filter | Grating Monochromator |
| **Number of Sample Beams** | 1 (Sample only) | 2 (Sample + Reference) | 2 (Sample + Reference) | 1 (Sample only) |
| **Source Compensation** | No | Yes (partial) | **Yes (complete, ratio-based)** | Yes (via reference channel) |
| **Output Quantity** | $I_f$ directly | $I_f - I_{ref}$ or ratio | $I_f \div I_{ref}$ (ratio) | Full spectrum |
| **Spectral Information** | Single fixed wavelength pair | Single fixed wavelength pair | Single fixed wavelength pair | **Excitation + Emission spectra** |
| **Typical Light Source** | Mercury Vapor Lamp (365 nm) | Mercury Vapor Lamp | Mercury or Xenon Arc | Xenon Arc Lamp (continuous spectrum) |
| **Detector** | Single PMT | Two PMTs (sample + ref) | Two PMTs (ratio computed) | PMT or CCD array |
| **Sensitivity** | High | High | Very High | Highest |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | Highest |
| **Major Application** | Routine clinical assays | Routine QC labs | High-precision research | Spectral fingerprinting, research |

### 3.3 Detailed Working — Single Beam Filter Fluorimeter

**Construction:**
- Light Source: Mercury vapor lamp (low-pressure, emits strong 254, 302, 313, **365 nm** lines) — most use 365 nm for quinine sulfate and similar analytes.
- Primary Filter ($\mathbf{F_1}$): Selects the excitation wavelength (e.g., 365 nm). Either an **interference filter** (narrow bandpass) or an **absorption filter** (colored glass).
- Sample Holder: Standard **1 cm × 1 cm × 4 cm** quartz cuvette (must be quartz, not glass, since glass absorbs UV).
- Secondary Filter ($\mathbf{F_2}$): Blocks residual excitation light and transmits only the emission wavelength (e.g., 460 nm for quinine).
- Detector: **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** — preferred for its high sensitivity and low dark current.

**Working Principle:**
1. Mercury lamp emits polychromatic UV light.
2. $\mathbf{F_1}$ transmits only the desired excitation wavelength.
3. Monochromatic excitation beam strikes the sample cuvette at 90° geometry.
4. Sample molecules absorb and re-emit fluorescence in all directions.
5. Detector positioned at 90° collects emitted light.
6. $\mathbf{F_2}$ filters out any scattered excitation light.
7. PMT converts fluorescence photons into electrical signal.
8. Signal is amplified and displayed.

**Limitations:**
- Source intensity fluctuations directly affect reading.
- No compensation for drift in lamp output or detector sensitivity.

### 3.4 Detailed Working — Double Beam Filter Fluorimeter

**Key Innovation**: The excitation beam is split into two paths using a **beam splitter** (typically a partially reflecting mirror or a quartz plate).

**Optical Path:**
- 50% of excitation beam → **Reference Cuvette** (containing a stable fluorescent reference standard like rhodamine B or a piece of fluorescent glass)
- 50% of excitation beam → **Sample Cuvette** (containing the analyte)
- The fluorescence from both cuvettes is measured by separate PMTs.
- The **difference** $(I_{sample} - I_{reference})$ is computed and displayed.

**Advantage over Single Beam:**
- Compensates for source intensity fluctuations, electronic drift, and detector aging.
- Provides a more stable baseline.

**Disadvantage:**
- Still measures **difference**, not ratio. Hence, absolute source fluctuations are partially compensated, but proportional errors remain.

### 3.5 Detailed Working — Ratio Fluorimeter

**Mathematical Concept:**
$$R = \frac{I_{sample}}{I_{reference}}$$

When source intensity fluctuates, both $I_{sample}$ AND $I_{reference}$ change by the same factor. The **ratio remains constant**, providing perfect compensation.

**Optical Configuration:**
- Source → Beam Splitter → Two excitation beams (both at same $\lambda_{ex}$).
- Sample beam → Sample Cuvette → Emission Filter → PMT-A (measures $I_f$).
- Reference beam → Reference Cuvette (stable fluorophore, e.g., **eosin** or **rhodamine B**) → PMT-B (measures $I_{ref}$).
- **Electronic Ratio Circuit**: Computes $I_{sample}/I_{reference}$ in real-time.
- Display shows the ratio, which is directly proportional to the sample concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept**: Why is the ratio fluorimeter more accurate than the double beam instrument? — Because the ratio operation is mathematically drift-free, while subtraction only removes the **DC component** of drift.

### 3.6 Detailed Working — Spectrofluorimeter

**Major Difference from Filter Fluorimeters:**
- Uses **two grating monochromators** instead of fixed filters.
- The excitation monochromator can be **scanned** across wavelengths to obtain the **excitation spectrum**.
- The emission monochromator can be **scanned** to obtain the **emission spectrum**.

**Components:**
- Light Source: **Xenon Arc Lamp** (continuous spectrum from 200 nm to 800 nm — superior to mercury which is line source).
- Excitation Monochromator: Czerny-Turner design with diffraction grating (typically 1200 lines/mm).
- Sample Compartment: With multiple cuvette holders, often temperature-controlled.
- Emission Monochromator: Identical Czerny-Turner design.
- Detector: PMT (sensitive single-point) or CCD array (for full-spectrum acquisition).
- Recorder/Computer: Plots intensity vs wavelength.

**Modes of Operation:**
1. **Emission Spectrum Mode**: Excitation monochromator fixed at $\lambda_{ex,max}$; emission monochromator scanned.
2. **Excitation Spectrum Mode**: Emission monochromator fixed at $\lambda_{em,max}$; excitation monochromator scanned.
3. **Synchronous Scan Mode**: Both monochromators scanned simultaneously with a fixed wavelength difference $\Delta\lambda$ equal to the Stokes Shift. Produces sharper spectra.

**Advantages over Filter Fluorimeters:**
- Records complete spectral information for identification.
- Allows selection of optimal $\lambda_{ex}$ and $\lambda_{em}$ for any analyte.
- Can resolve overlapping spectra of mixtures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Jablonski Energy Level Diagram (Fluorescence Process)

The Jablonski diagram illustrates the electronic transitions occurring during fluorescence. Mermaid is used to represent the energy levels and transitions since this is the standard physics textbook schematic.

```mermaid
graph TB
    S0["GROUND STATE S0"]:::ground
    S1Vib["S1 Vibrational Levels"]:::vib
    S1["EXCITED STATE S1"]:::excited
    S2["Higher Excited State S2"]:::excited
    
    A1["Absorption: h nu_ex 1"]:::absorb
    A2["Internal Conversion"]:::nonrad
    A3["Vibrational Relaxation"]:::nonrad
    A4["FLUORESCENCE: h nu_em"]:::emit
    
    S2 -->|A1: Absorption| S1Vib
    S1Vib -->|A3: Vibrational Relaxation| S1
    S1 -->|A4: Fluorescence Emission| S0
    S2 -.->|A2: Internal Conversion| S1Vib
    
    classDef ground fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef excited fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vib fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef absorb fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef emit fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nonrad fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Single Beam Filter Fluorimeter

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["Mercury Vapor Lamp Source"]:::source
    F1["Primary Filter F1 selects lambda_ex"]:::filter
    LENS1["Lens Collimates Beam"]:::optics
    SAMP["Sample Cuvette Quartz"]:::sample
    F2["Secondary Filter F2 selects lambda_em"]:::filter
    PMT["Photomultiplier Tube PMT"]:::detector
    AMP["Amplifier"]:::signal
    DISP["Readout Display"]:::output
    
    SRC ==> F1
    F1 ==> LENS1
    LENS1 ==> SAMP
    SAMP -.->|Fluorescence 90 degrees| F2
    F2 ==> PMT
    PMT ==> AMP
    AMP ==> DISP
    
    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef filter fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef optics fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sample fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detector fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signal fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Double Beam Filter Fluorimeter

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["Mercury Vapor Lamp"]:::source
    BS["Beam Splitter 50:50"]:::optics
    F1S["Filter F1S lambda_ex"]:::filter
    F1R["Filter F1R lambda_ex"]:::filter
    SC["Sample Cuvette"]:::sample
    RC["Reference Cuvette Stable Fluorophore"]:::sample
    F2S["Filter F2S lambda_em Sample"]:::filter
    F2R["Filter F2R lambda_em Reference"]:::filter
    PMTS["PMT Sample Channel"]:::detector
    PMTR["PMT Reference Channel"]:::detector
    DIFF["Subtraction Circuit"]:::signal
    DISP["Display Isample minus Iref"]:::output
    
    SRC ==> BS
    BS ==> F1S
    BS ==> F1R
    F1S ==> SC
    F1R ==> RC
    SC -.->|Fluorescence| F2S
    RC -.->|Fluorescence| F2R
    F2S ==> PMTS
    F2R ==> PMTR
    PMTS ==> DIFF
    PMTR ==> DIFF
    DIFF ==> DISP
    
    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef optics fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px
    classDef filter fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sample fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detector fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signal fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Ratio Fluorimeter

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["Xenon or Mercury Lamp"]:::source
    BS["Beam Splitter"]:::optics
    FEX["Excitation Filter"]:::filter
    SC["Sample Cuvette"]:::sample
    RC["Reference Fluorophore Cell"]:::sample
    FEM["Emission Filter"]:::filter
    PMTS["PMT A Sample Detector"]:::detector
    PMTR["PMT B Reference Detector"]:::detector
    RATIO["Electronic Ratio Circuit Computes Isample divided by Iref"]:::signal
    DISP["Display Ratio Signal"]:::output
    
    SRC ==> BS
    BS ==> FEX
    FEX ==> SC
    FEX ==> RC
    SC -.->|Fluorescence| FEM
    FEM ==> PMTS
    RC -.->|Reference Fluorescence| PMTR
    PMTS ==> RATIO
    PMTR ==> RATIO
    RATIO ==> DISP
    
    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef optics fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px
    classDef filter fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sample fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detector fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signal fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Spectrofluorimeter (Dual Monochromator)

```mermaid
graph TB
    XE["Xenon Arc Lamp Continuous UV Vis 200 to 800 nm"]:::source
    M1["Excitation Monochromator Grating Scannable"]:::mono
    SLIT1["Entrance Slit"]:::optics
    SC["Sample Compartment Temp Controlled"]:::sample
    M2["Emission Monochromator Grating Scannable"]:::mono
    SLIT2["Exit Slit"]:::optics
    PMT["PMT or CCD Detector"]:::detector
    AMP["Amplifier and ADC"]:::signal
    CPU["Computer Generates Excitation and Emission Spectra"]:::output
    
    XE ==> M1
    M1 ==> SLIT1
    SLIT1 ==> SC
    SC -.->|Fluorescence 90 degrees| M2
    M2 ==> SLIT2
    SLIT2 ==> PMT
    PMT ==> AMP
    AMP ==> CPU
    
    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mono fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef optics fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sample fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detector fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef signal fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.6 Geometric Comparison: Detector Placement at 90°

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Right-Angle Geometry Fluorimeter"
        BEAM["Excitation Beam horizontal direction"]:::beam
        SAMP["Sample Cell"]:::sample
        DET["Detector at 90 degrees"]:::detector
        BEAM --> SAMP
        SAMP -.->|Fluorescence emission isotropic| DET
    end
    
    classDef beam fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    classDef sample fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef detector fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why 90° Geometry?** Placing the detector perpendicular to the excitation beam prevents transmitted excitation light from reaching the detector. This is critical because fluorescence intensity is orders of magnitude weaker than excitation intensity, and any leakage would saturate the PMT.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define fluorimetry. What is Stokes Shift and why is it essential for fluorescence measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fluorimetry is an analytical technique that measures the intensity of fluorescent light emitted by a substance after it absorbs ultraviolet or visible radiation.

**Stokes Shift** is defined as the difference between the wavelengths of emitted and absorbed radiation:

$$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_{emission} - \lambda_{excitation} > 0$$

It is essential because it allows **geometric separation** of excitation and emission light using filters or monochromators. The emitted fluorescence always occurs at a longer wavelength than the excitation, enabling the detector to distinguish fluorescence from the much stronger scattered excitation light. Without a Stokes shift, fluorescence could not be selectively measured.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Stokes Shift equation, 1 mark for its role in separation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three differences between a filter fluorimeter and a spectrofluorimeter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per difference):**

| S.No | Filter Fluorimeter | Spectrofluorimeter |
|------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Uses **fixed-wavelength optical filters** for wavelength selection | Uses **scannable grating monochromators** for wavelength selection |
| 2 | Can measure fluorescence intensity at **only one fixed wavelength pair** | Can record the **complete excitation and emission spectra** |
| 3 | Uses a **mercury vapor lamp** (line source) as light source | Uses a **xenon arc lamp** (continuous spectrum source) |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Single Beam Filter Fluorimeter**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between fluorescence intensity and analyte concentration for a dilute solution. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Single Beam Filter Fluorimeter (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The instrument consists of the following components arranged in a linear optical path:

1. **Light Source**: A **low-pressure mercury vapor lamp** is used. It emits strong characteristic lines at 254 nm, 302 nm, 313 nm, and 365 nm. The 365 nm line is most commonly employed.

2. **Primary Filter (F₁)**: An interference filter or colored glass absorption filter placed immediately after the source. It isolates the desired excitation wavelength (e.g., 365 nm).

3. **Lens System**: A quartz lens collimates the filtered excitation beam and focuses it onto the sample cuvette.

4. **Sample Compartment**: Holds a **1 cm path length quartz cuvette** containing the fluorescent analyte in solution. Quartz is used because it transmits UV radiation unlike ordinary glass.

5. **Secondary Filter (F₂)**: Positioned between the sample and the detector at 90° to the excitation beam. It blocks any residual excitation light and transmits only the emission wavelength.

6. **Detector**: A **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** is used for its high sensitivity (current amplification of 10⁶ to 10⁷) and low dark current.

7. **Amplifier and Readout**: The PMT signal is amplified and displayed on a meter or recorder.

**Working (2 marks):**

The mercury lamp emits polychromatic UV radiation. The primary filter selects the excitation wavelength $\lambda_{ex}$, which is focused onto the sample cuvette. Fluorophore molecules absorb photons and re-emit fluorescence at a longer wavelength $\lambda_{em}$ in all directions. The detector placed at **90°** to the excitation beam collects this fluorescence. The secondary filter ensures only $\lambda_{em}$ reaches the PMT, which converts it to an electrical signal proportional to concentration.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Simple, robust, and inexpensive.
- High sensitivity (detection limits in ppb range).
- Easy to operate for routine clinical and environmental assays.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Cannot compensate for fluctuations in source intensity.
- Provides only single fixed wavelength measurement — no spectral information.
- Cannot resolve mixtures of fluorophores.

---

**(b) Derivation of Fluorescence Intensity vs Concentration (7 marks)**

**Starting Equation (Beer-Lambert Law) (1 mark):**

$$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon b c}$$

**Absorbed Intensity (1 mark):**

$$I_{abs} = I_0 - I = I_0 \left(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon b c}\right)$$

**Multiplication by Quantum Yield (1 mark):**

$$I_f = \phi_f \cdot I_0 \left(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon b c}\right)$$

**Taylor Series Expansion for Small $\varepsilon b c$ (2 marks):**

For dilute solutions, $\varepsilon b c \le 0.02$. Using $10^{-x} \approx 1 - 2.303 \cdot x$:

$$I_f = \phi_f \cdot I_0 \left[1 - (1 - 2.303 \cdot \varepsilon b c)\right]$$

$$I_f = 2.303 \cdot \phi_f \cdot I_0 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$$

**Final Result and Assumptions (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{I_f \propto c}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The solution is **dilute** ($\varepsilon b c \le 0.02$) so higher-order terms are negligible.
2. The incident intensity $I_0$ is constant.
3. There is **no inner filter effect** (no self-absorption of emitted fluorescence).
4. The quantum yield $\phi_f$ is independent of concentration (no quenching or aggregation).
5. No scattering or stray light contamination.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Spectrofluorimeter**. Explain the difference between excitation and emission spectra. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Ratio Fluorimeter** principle. How does it overcome the limitations of single and double beam filter fluorimeters? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Spectrofluorimeter (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A spectrofluorimeter consists of:

1. **Light Source**: A **high-pressure xenon arc lamp** providing continuous radiation from **200 nm to 800 nm**. This is essential because it allows any excitation wavelength to be selected.

2. **Excitation Monochromator**: A **Czerny-Turner grating monochromator** with an entrance slit, collimating mirror, rotatable diffraction grating (typically 1200 lines/mm), and exit slit. The grating can be rotated to scan wavelengths.

3. **Sample Compartment**: Holds a quartz cuvette, often temperature-controlled to ±0.1°C to eliminate thermal fluctuations in fluorescence.

4. **Emission Monochromator**: Identical Czerny-Turner design as the excitation monochromator. Placed at 90° to the excitation path.

5. **Detector**: Either a **PMT** (for scanning) or a **CCD array** (for simultaneous multi-wavelength detection).

6. **Data System**: Computer-controlled with software for spectral acquisition, baseline correction, and quantitative analysis.

**Working — Two Scanning Modes (2 marks):**

- **Emission Spectrum**: Excitation monochromator is fixed at $\lambda_{ex,max}$ (the wavelength that gives maximum fluorescence). The emission monochromator is then scanned across wavelengths, plotting intensity vs $\lambda_{em}$. This yields the **fluorescence emission spectrum**.

- **Excitation Spectrum**: Emission monochromator is fixed at $\lambda_{em,max}$. The excitation monochromator is scanned. This yields the **excitation spectrum**, which closely resembles the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore.

**Difference Between Spectra (2 marks):**

| Property | Excitation Spectrum | Emission Spectrum |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Scanning Element | Excitation monochromator varies | Emission monochromator varies |
| Fixed Element | $\lambda_{em}$ fixed at maximum | $\lambda_{ex}$ fixed at maximum |
| Information Content | Resembles absorption spectrum | Unique fingerprint of fluorophore |
| Useful For | Selecting best excitation wavelength | Identification and quantification |

---

**(b) Ratio Fluorimeter Principle (7 marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):**

The output is the **ratio** of two fluorescence signals:

$$R = \frac{I_{sample}}{I_{reference}}$$

When source intensity $I_0$ fluctuates by a factor $k$, both numerator and denominator change by the same factor $k$ (since they are derived from the same source). Therefore:

$$R = \frac{k \cdot I_{sample,0}}{k \cdot I_{reference,0}} = \text{constant}$$

**Construction (2 marks):**

- Source → Beam Splitter → Two parallel excitation beams.
- One beam excites the **sample**; the other excites a **reference fluorophore** (stable inorganic phosphor or rhodamine B solution).
- Two PMTs measure $I_{sample}$ and $I_{reference}$ separately.
- An **electronic ratio circuit** computes $R = I_{sample}/I_{reference}$ in real-time.
- The display shows $R$, which is directly proportional to sample concentration.

**How It Overcomes Other Fluorimeters' Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Compared to Single Beam**: Single beam instruments have NO compensation for source drift. Any change in lamp output is misinterpreted as a change in concentration. The ratio circuit cancels this effect.

2. **Compared to Double Beam**: The double beam measures the **difference** $I_{sample} - I_{reference}$. If the source drifts by factor $k$, both signals scale, and the **difference** still changes:
   $$\Delta I = k I_{sample,0} - k I_{reference,0} = k(I_{sample,0} - I_{reference,0})$$
   So $\Delta I$ scales with $k$ — drift is NOT fully cancelled. The ratio operation cancels drift completely.

3. **Result**: The ratio fluorimeter provides superior long-term stability, accuracy, and reproducibility. It is the instrument of choice for **high-precision analytical work**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Fluorimetry Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Filters and Monochromators**: Many students incorrectly interchange these terms. Filters are **fixed-wavelength, low-resolution** devices used in filter fluorimeters. Monochromators are **scannable, high-resolution** devices used in spectrofluorimeters. (3-mark penalty possible)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 90° Geometry**: When asked to draw a fluorimeter block diagram, students often place the detector in line with the excitation beam. **Always place it at 90°** to avoid excitation light contamination. (Up to 2 marks lost)
>
> 3. **Missing the Quantum Yield in the Formula**: Writing $I_f = I_0(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon b c})$ without $\phi_f$ loses 1 mark. The quantum yield is essential.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to State Assumptions** in derivations: The dilution assumption $\varepsilon b c \le 0.02$ and the absence of inner filter effect MUST be stated explicitly. Examiners specifically look for these.
>
> 5. **Confusing "Difference" with "Ratio"** in double beam vs. ratio fluorimeter questions: This is the most commonly tested conceptual distinction. Clearly state that **ratio fluorimeters compute $I_s/I_r$**, while double beam computes $I_s - I_r$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- **Fluorimetry** measures fluorescence (emission of light at $\lambda_{em} > \lambda_{ex}$).
- **Stokes Shift**: $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_{em} - \lambda_{ex} > 0$ — always positive.
- **Fluorescence Lifetime**: $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-7}$ seconds.
- **General Equation**: $I_f = \phi_f \cdot I_0 \left(1 - 10^{-\varepsilon b c}\right)$.
- **Dilute Solution Equation**: $I_f = 2.303 \cdot \phi_f \cdot I_0 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot c$ (linear in $c$).
- **Beer-Lambert**: $A = \varepsilon b c$.
- **Quantum Yield**: $\phi_f = \dfrac{k_f}{k_f + k_{nr}}$ (range: 0 to 1).
- **Single Beam Filter Fluorimeter**: One beam, one filter pair, mercury lamp — simplest instrument.
- **Double Beam Filter Fluorimeter**: Two beams, two filter pairs, computes difference — partial drift compensation.
- **Ratio Fluorimeter**: Two beams, computes **ratio** — perfect drift compensation.
- **Spectrofluorimeter**: Two **monochromators**, xenon lamp, records excitation AND emission spectra.
- **Light Sources**: Filter fluorimeters use mercury vapor; spectrofluorimeters use xenon arc.
- **Detector**: PMT (Photomultiplier Tube) — standard for all variants.
- **Geometry**: Detector is always at **90°** to excitation beam.
- **Cuvette Material**: Quartz (transparent to UV); never glass for UV work.
- **Order of Sensitivity**: Spectrofluorimeter > Ratio Fluorimeter > Double Beam > Single Beam.
- **Fluorimetry vs Spectrophotometry**: Fluorimetry is **100–1000× more sensitive** because it measures emission above a dark background, while absorption measures a small difference between two large signals.
- **Inner Filter Effect**: At high concentrations, fluorescence linearity breaks down due to self-absorption.
- **Stokes Shift Example**: Quinine sulfate absorbs at 365 nm and emits at 460 nm — $\Delta\lambda = 95$ nm.
- **Common Analytes**: Quinine sulfate, fluorescein, rhodamine B, chlorophyll, aromatic amino acids (tryptophan, tyrosine), vitamins (A, B₂, E).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation, Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometers, Components of a Mass Spectrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT862 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of Operation, Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometers, Components of a Mass Spectrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mass Spectrometer – Formal Definition

A **Mass Spectrometer** is a highly sensitive analytical instrument used to determine the **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** of ionized atoms, molecules, or molecular fragments. By measuring these ratios with extreme precision, the instrument can identify unknown compounds, quantify known materials, and elucidate the chemical structure of molecules.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A mass spectrometer does NOT measure mass directly. It measures the **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** of ions moving through electric and/or magnetic fields. This is the foundational concept behind every sub-topic in Module 3.

## 1.2 Working Principle – At a Glance

The universal operating principle of a mass spectrometer is built on **four sequential stages**, regardless of the type:

1. **Sample Introduction** – A tiny quantity of the analyte is admitted into a high-vacuum environment.
2. **Ionization** – Neutral atoms/molecules are converted into ions (typically positive ions).
3. **Separation / Mass Analysis** – Ions are separated in space or time according to their m/z ratio.
4. **Detection** – The separated ions strike a detector, producing an electrical signal proportional to their abundance.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy – The "Sorting Machine" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **marble sorting machine** in a factory. Marbles of identical size but different weights are dropped onto a curved conveyor. Lighter marbles follow a sharp inner curve, while heavier marbles follow a wider outer curve. By observing which collection bin each marble lands in, we can determine its weight without ever placing it on a scale. A mass spectrometer does the same — but instead of marbles, it sorts **charged particles** using invisible **magnetic and electric forces**.

## 1.4 Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometer – Definition

A **Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometer** (also called a **Magnetic Sector Mass Spectrometer**) is a type of mass analyzer in which a **uniform magnetic field** deflects a beam of accelerated ions along a circular path. Ions of different m/z values follow paths of **different radii**, allowing them to be spatially separated and detected.

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| m | Mass of ion | kilogram (kg) |
| z | Charge number | dimensionless |
| q = z·e | Charge of ion | Coulomb (C) |
| V | Accelerating voltage | Volt (V) |
| B | Magnetic flux density | Tesla (T) |
| r | Radius of deflection | meter (m) |
| v | Velocity of ion | m/s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Principle of a Magnetic Sector Instrument

The operational logic can be broken into **six rigorous physics-based steps**:

### Step 1 — Sample Vaporization
The liquid or gaseous sample is converted into a low-pressure molecular vapor inside a vacuum chamber. High vacuum is essential because collisions with residual gas molecules would scatter the ion beam and destroy resolution.

### Step 2 — Ionization
The vapor passes through an **ion source** (commonly an **Electron Impact – EI** source operating at **70 eV**). A heated filament emits electrons that collide with sample molecules, knocking out an electron and producing a **positive molecular ion** (radical cation):

$$M + e^- \rightarrow M^{+\bullet} + 2e^-$$

### Step 3 — Acceleration
The newly formed positive ions are extracted and accelerated through a potential difference **V** (typically 1 kV – 10 kV). The work done by the electric field equals the kinetic energy gained by the ion:

$$qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

### Step 4 — Magnetic Deflection (Mass Analysis)
The accelerated ion beam enters a sector-shaped region subjected to a uniform magnetic field **B** perpendicular to the ion velocity. The magnetic Lorentz force provides centripetal force, bending the ion into a circular trajectory:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

### Step 5 — Spatial Separation by m/z
Solving the above for the radius **r**:

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

Because each ion has a specific m/z, each follows a **unique circular path**. Only ions whose trajectory matches the radius of the analyzer tube reach the detector. By scanning either **B** or **V**, ions of different masses can be brought into focus one after another (mass scanning).

### Step 6 — Detection
The ion strikes a detector (Faraday cup or electron multiplier), generating a measurable current. This current vs. mass-to-charge value is plotted as the **mass spectrum**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $qV = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Energy gained during acceleration | J, J |
| 2 | $v = \sqrt{2qV/m}$ | Velocity after acceleration | m/s |
| 3 | $qvB = mv^2/r$ | Lorentz force = Centripetal force | N |
| 4 | $r = \dfrac{1}{B}\sqrt{\dfrac{2mV}{q}}$ | Radius of ion trajectory in B-field | m |
| 5 | $\dfrac{m}{q} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2V}$ | **Master Equation** — m/z from B, r, V | kg/C |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{r}{s_1 + s_2}$ | Resolving power of single-focus sector | dimensionless |
| 7 | $B_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2Vm_{max}}{qr^2}}$ | Maximum B-field for heaviest ion | Tesla |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation #5 is the heart of every magnetic deflection problem. Master it — KTU examiners will supply three of the four variables (m, q, B, V, r) and ask for the fourth.

## 2.3 The Three Major Functional Components (Conceptual Overview)

A mass spectrometer consists of three **macro-modules**:

| Macro-Component | Function | Common Implementations |
|---|---|---|
| **Inlet + Ion Source** | Introduce sample; convert to ions | Electron Impact, Chemical Ionization, MALDI |
| **Mass Analyzer** | Separate ions by m/z | Magnetic sector, Quadrupole, TOF, Ion trap |
| **Detector + Recorder** | Count ions; record spectrum | Faraday cup, Electron Multiplier, Micro-channel plate |

A **vacuum system** and a **data system** are auxiliary but indispensable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Equation (m/q relationship)

We begin from first principles using Newton's second law and the Lorentz force law.

### Given Data / Assumptions
- A singly charged ion of mass **m** and charge **q = e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** is accelerated through potential **V**.
- It then enters a uniform magnetic field **B** perpendicular to its velocity.
- The ion travels in a circular path of radius **r**.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy balance in the accelerating region:**

The electrical potential energy lost by the ion equals its kinetic energy gained.

$$
qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2
$$

**Step 2 — Rearranging for velocity:**

Divide both sides by (m/2):

$$
\frac{2qV}{m} = v^2
$$

Take the square root of both sides:

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Force balance inside the magnetic field:**

The magnetic force on a moving charge is **F = qvB**, and for circular motion this must equal the centripetal force **mv²/r**:

$$
qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the radius r:**

Cancel one factor of v from both sides:

$$
qB = \frac{mv}{r}
$$

Multiply both sides by r:

$$
qrB = mv
$$

Divide both sides by qB:

$$
r = \frac{mv}{qB}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute v from Step 2:**

$$
r = \frac{m}{qB}\sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}
$$

Square both sides to eliminate the square root:

$$
r^2 = \frac{m^2}{q^2 B^2} \cdot \frac{2qV}{m}
$$

Cancel one m and one q:

$$
r^2 = \frac{2mV}{qB^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Master Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{m}{q} = \frac{B^2 r^2}{2V}}
$$

This is the **single most important equation** for magnetic sector mass spectrometers in the KTU syllabus.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A singly charged ion (m/q ≈ mass of a proton = 1.673 × 10⁻²⁷ kg/C) is accelerated through 5000 V and travels in a circular path of radius 0.10 m inside a magnetic field. Calculate the magnetic flux density B.

### Solution

**Step 1 — List given values:**

- $\dfrac{m}{q} = 1.673 \times 10^{-27}$ kg/C
- V = 5000 V
- r = 0.10 m

**Step 2 — Rearrange the master equation for B:**

$$
\frac{m}{q} = \frac{B^2 r^2}{2V}
$$

$$
B^2 = \frac{2V(m/q)}{r^2}
$$

$$
B = \sqrt{\frac{2V(m/q)}{r^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
B = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000 \times 1.673 \times 10^{-27}}{(0.10)^2}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
2 \times 5000 \times 1.673 \times 10^{-27} = 1.673 \times 10^{-23}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
(0.10)^2 = 0.01 = 10^{-2}
$$

Divide:

$$
\frac{1.673 \times 10^{-23}}{10^{-2}} = 1.673 \times 10^{-21}
$$

Take square root:

$$
B = \sqrt{1.673 \times 10^{-21}} = 4.09 \times 10^{-11} \text{ T}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is unrealistically low for a real instrument, indicating the chosen numbers were pedagogical. In a real magnetic sector instrument, **B** ranges from **0.4 T to 1.5 T**, and **V** from **1000 V to 10000 V**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Components of a Mass Spectrometer

| # | Component | Sub-Component | Function | Typical Specification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Inlet System** | Direct probe | Solid/liquid insertion | Probe tip at 10⁻⁶ Pa |
| 1 | **Sample Inlet System** | Heated reservoir | Volatile liquids/gases | Up to 300 °C |
| 1 | **Sample Inlet System** | GC/MS interface | Coupled gas chromatography | He carrier, 1 mL/min |
| 2 | **Ion Source** | Electron Impact (EI) | Hard ionization | 70 eV electrons |
| 2 | **Ion Source** | Chemical Ionization (CI) | Soft ionization via reagent gas | CH₄, NH₃, isobutane |
| 2 | **Ion Source** | Electrospray (ESI) | Liquid phase ionization | 2–5 kV needle |
| 3 | **Mass Analyzer** | Magnetic Sector | High-resolution, m/q separation | r = 0.05–2 m |
| 3 | **Mass Analyzer** | Quadrupole | Compact, fast scanning | 4 rods, RF+DC |
| 3 | **Mass Analyzer** | Time-of-Flight (TOF) | Unlimited mass range | Drift length 0.5–2 m |
| 4 | **Detector** | Faraday Cup | Absolute ion counting | 10⁻¹⁵ A sensitivity |
| 4 | **Detector** | Electron Multiplier | Amplification by dynodes | Gain 10⁶–10⁸ |
| 4 | **Detector** | Micro-channel Plate | Spatial + temporal detection | 10² channels |
| 5 | **Vacuum System** | Rotary + Diffusion/Turbo pumps | Maintain low pressure | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁷ Pa |
| 6 | **Data System** | ADC + Computer | Convert current → spectrum | 16–24 bit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Examiners often ask students to **draw and label the block diagram** of a mass spectrometer. Memorize the six components in the exact order: *Inlet → Source → Analyzer → Detector → Vacuum → Data System*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Full Mass Spectrometer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample Inlet System] --> B[Ion Source]
    B --> C[Accelerating Plates V]
    C --> D[Magnetic Sector Analyzer]
    D --> E[Ion Detector]
    E --> F[Amplifier and ADC]
    F --> G[Data System and Display]
    H[Vacuum Pump System] -.-> A
    H -.-> B
    H -.-> D
    H -.-> E
    classDef hw fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#e2ece9,stroke:#3a5a40,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7a6c0d,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E hw
    class F,G sw
    class H aux
```

## 4.2 Detailed Schematic — Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[Sample] --> IS[Ion Source Filament]
    IS -- Electrons e- --> IS2[Positive Ions Formed]
    IS2 --> AP[Accelerating Plates V kV]
    AP -- High velocity ion beam --> SLIT1[Entrance Slit S1]
    SLIT1 --> MAG[Magnetic Field B perpendicular to beam]
    MAG -- Curved trajectory radius r --> SLIT2[Exit Slit S2]
    SLIT2 --> DET[Detector Faraday Cup]
    DET --> AMP[Electrometer Amplifier]
    AMP --> REC[Recorder or PC]
    MAG -. Variable B supply .-> CTRL[Field Controller]
    CTRL -. Scan B .-> MAG
    classDef ion fill:#caf0f8,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000
    classDef mag fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#9d4edd,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#1b4332,color:#000
    class SA,IS,IS2,AP,SLIT1,SLIT2 ion
    class MAG,CTRL mag
    class DET,AMP,REC out
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Ion Path Inside Magnetic Sector

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VAC["High Vacuum Region 10 to the minus 5 Pa"]
        direction TB
        ION[Positively Charged Ion] -- accelerated by --> ACC[Potential V volts]
        ACC -- kinetic energy half m v squared --> VEL[Velocity v equals root 2 q V over m]
        VEL -- enters at 90 degrees --> MAGB[Uniform Magnetic Field B Tesla]
        MAGB -- Lorentz force q v B --> CIRC[Circular Path Radius r]
        CIRC -- arrives at exit slit --> OUT[Detector]
    end
    OUT --> SIG[Signal Current vs B Field]
    SIG --> SPEC[Mass Spectrum m by q axis]
    classDef stage fill:#e0e1dd,stroke:#415a77,color:#000
    class ION,ACC,VEL,MAGB,CIRC,OUT,SPEC stage
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Each node in the Mermaid diagrams corresponds to a real physical region inside the instrument. The arrow labels show the **equation** governing that stage, mirroring the derivation in Section 3.1.

## 4.4 Component Hierarchy — Macro to Micro View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS[Mass Spectrometer] --> S1[Sample Handling Module]
    MS --> S2[Ion Formation Module]
    MS --> S3[Mass Analysis Module]
    MS --> S4[Detection and Recording Module]
    MS --> S5[Vacuum and Support Module]
    S1 --> S1a[Direct Inlet Probe]
    S1 --> S1b[Chromatographic Interface]
    S2 --> S2a[Electron Impact Source]
    S2 --> S2b[Chemical Ionization Source]
    S2 --> S2c[Electrospray Ionization]
    S3 --> S3a[Magnetic Sector]
    S3 --> S3b[Quadrupole Filter]
    S3 --> S3c[Time of Flight Tube]
    S3 --> S3d[Ion Trap]
    S4 --> S4a[Faraday Cup]
    S4 --> S4b[Electron Multiplier]
    S4 --> S4c[Microchannel Plate]
    S5 --> S5a[Rotary Pump]
    S5 --> S5b[Turbo Pump]
    S5 --> S5c[Vacuum Gauge]
    S5 --> S5d[Power Supply Unit]

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 lvl1
```
