<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
Define **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)**. Why is it the fundamental quantity measured in mass spectrometry rather than mass alone?

**Model Answer:**
The mass-to-charge ratio, denoted m/z, is the dimensionless (or kg/C) ratio of an ion's mass to its charge number. It is fundamental because in any mass analyzer, the ion's response to electric and magnetic fields depends on **both** m and q simultaneously. Two ions with identical m but different z would follow different trajectories, and two ions with the same m/z would be indistinguishable. Hence, mass spectrometers are calibrated in m/z, not in mass alone. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
List any **four essential components** of a mass spectrometer and state the function of each in one line.

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Ion Source | Converts neutral sample molecules into ions |
| 2. Mass Analyzer | Separates ions according to m/z ratio |
| 3. Detector | Converts arriving ions into an electrical signal |
| 4. Vacuum System | Maintains low pressure (~10⁻⁵ Pa) to avoid ion-molecule collisions |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget the SI units** of m and q and lose 1 mark.
> - Many write the Lorentz force as `F = qv × B` without stating that B is **perpendicular** to v (a mandatory assumption for circular motion).
> - In derivation questions, skipping the "cancel one m and one q" step loses **2 marks**.
> - Block diagram questions: forgetting the **vacuum system** is a common deduction of **1 mark**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**A(a).** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a magnetic deflection mass spectrometer**. List all major components and state their functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A magnetic deflection mass spectrometer separates ions of different mass-to-charge ratio by passing them through a magnetic field perpendicular to their direction of motion. Ions of different m/z follow circular paths of different radii, enabling spatial separation.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Sample → Inlet → Ion Source → Accelerating Plates → Magnetic Sector → Detector → Recorder
                                                     ↑
                                              (Uniform B field)
```

**Component-wise Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Sample Inlet System:** Introduces microgram-quantity sample into vacuum via direct probe, reservoir, or chromatographic interface.
2. **Ion Source (Electron Impact):** A tungsten filament emits 70 eV electrons which ionize the sample: $M + e^- \rightarrow M^{+\bullet} + 2e^-$
3. **Accelerating Plates:** Positively charged ions are accelerated through potential V (typically 1–10 kV) acquiring kinetic energy qV.
4. **Magnetic Sector Analyzer:** A uniform magnetic field B (0.4–1.5 T) bends the ion beam into a circular arc of radius r given by $r = \frac{1}{B}\sqrt{\frac{2mV}{q}}$.
5. **Detector (Faraday cup / Electron multiplier):** Converts the arriving ion current into a measurable electrical signal.
6. **Vacuum System:** Maintains pressure of 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁷ Pa throughout the ion path.
7. **Data System:** Plots ion current versus m/z to produce the mass spectrum.

**Conclusion:** The magnetic sector offers high resolution (R > 100,000 in double-focusing designs) and is widely used in isotope ratio analysis and high-resolution organic mass spectrometry.

---

**A(b).** Derive the expression **m/q = B²r² / (2V)** for a magnetic deflection mass spectrometer, explaining each step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Acceleration Equation (2 marks):**
The work done on a charge q accelerated through potential V equals its kinetic energy:

$$
qV = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity Expression (1 mark):**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}} \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 3 — Force Balance in Magnetic Field (2 marks):**
Inside the magnetic sector, the magnetic Lorentz force supplies the centripetal force:

$$
qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r} \quad \text{...(iii)}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Radius r (1 mark):**

$$
r = \frac{mv}{qB} \quad \text{...(iv)}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitution and Final Equation (1 mark):**

Substitute (ii) into (iv):

$$
r = \frac{m}{qB}\sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
r^2 = \frac{m^2}{q^2B^2} \cdot \frac{2qV}{m} = \frac{2mV}{qB^2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\frac{m}{q} = \frac{B^2 r^2}{2V}}
$$

This is the **fundamental equation** of a magnetic sector mass spectrometer. It relates the m/z of an ion directly to the experimentally controllable quantities B, V, and r. **(7 marks total)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**B(a).** A singly charged ion of mass 2.0 × 10⁻²⁵ kg is accelerated through 4000 V and enters a uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T perpendicular to its motion. Calculate the **radius of its circular trajectory**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- m = 2.0 × 10⁻²⁵ kg
- q = e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- V = 4000 V
- B = 0.5 T

**Step 1 — Calculate velocity from acceleration equation (2 marks):**

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2qV}{m}}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 4000}{2.0 \times 10^{-25}}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 4000 = 1.2816 \times 10^{-15}
$$

Divide by m:

$$
\frac{1.2816 \times 10^{-15}}{2.0 \times 10^{-25}} = 6.408 \times 10^{9}
$$

Take square root:

$$
v = \sqrt{6.408 \times 10^{9}} = 8.005 \times 10^{4} \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply magnetic force equation (2 marks):**

$$
r = \frac{mv}{qB}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve (2 marks):**

$$
r = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-25} \times 8.005 \times 10^{4}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5}
$$

Numerator:

$$
2.0 \times 10^{-25} \times 8.005 \times 10^{4} = 1.601 \times 10^{-20}
$$

Denominator:

$$
1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 = 8.01 \times 10^{-20}
$$

Divide:

$$
r = \frac{1.601 \times 10^{-20}}{8.01 \times 10^{-20}} = 0.1999 \approx 0.20 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** The radius of the circular trajectory is **r ≈ 0.20 m (20 cm)**. **(7 marks)**

---

**B(b).** Explain the **components of a mass spectrometer** with the help of a block diagram and describe the function of each component in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐   ┌──────────────┐
│Sample Inlet  │ → │  Ion Source  │ → │Mass Analyzer │ → │  Detector    │
│   System     │   │  (EI / CI)   │   │  (Magnetic / │   │ (Faraday /   │
│              │   │              │   │  Quadrupole) │   │  EM)         │
└──────────────┘   └──────────────┘   └──────────────┘   └──────────────┘
       ↑                                                          ↓
       ←────── Vacuum System (10⁻⁵ Pa) ─────────  Data System ────→
```

**Component Description (5 marks):**

1. **Sample Inlet System (1 mark):** Accepts the analyte (gas, liquid, or solid) and delivers it as a low-pressure vapor into the ion source. Types include reservoir inlets, direct insertion probes, and chromatographic interfaces (GC/MS, LC/MS).

2. **Ion Source (1 mark):** Converts neutral sample molecules into ions. The most common is Electron Impact (EI), operating at 70 eV. Other types include Chemical Ionization (CI), Electrospray Ionization (ESI), and Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption Ionization (MALDI).

3. **Mass Analyzer (1 mark):** The heart of the instrument. It separates ions according to their m/z ratio using electric fields, magnetic fields, or flight time. Common types: magnetic sector (high resolution), quadrupole (robust, fast), time-of-flight (TOF, unlimited mass range).

4. **Detector (1 mark):** Converts the flux of arriving ions into a measurable electrical signal. Faraday cups provide absolute measurement; electron multipliers (dynode chain) amplify single-ion events by 10⁶–10⁸; microchannel plates enable spatial detection.

5. **Vacuum System + Data System (1 mark):** The vacuum (typically 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁷ Pa via rotary + turbo pumps) prevents ion scattering and neutralization. The data system digitizes the detector output and plots the **mass spectrum** (intensity vs. m/z).

**Conclusion:** The synergy of these components gives mass spectrometry its extraordinary sensitivity (down to attomole levels) and precision (ppm mass accuracy in high-resolution instruments). **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Magnetic Deflection Mass Spectrometry**

- **Core Principle:** Measure **m/z** of ions, NOT mass alone.
- **Two governing equations** to memorize:
  - Acceleration: $qV = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$
  - Deflection: $qvB = mv^2/r$
- **Master Equation:** $\dfrac{m}{q} = \dfrac{B^2 r^2}{2V}$
- **Six components in order:** Inlet → Source → Analyzer → Detector → Vacuum → Data System.
- **Ion Source (EI) standard energy:** **70 eV** (memorize this number).
- **Vacuum requirement:** 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁷ Pa (mention units if asked).
- **Magnetic field range in real instruments:** **0.4 T – 1.5 T**.
- **Common ion sources:** EI, CI, ESI, MALDI, APCI.
- **Common mass analyzers:** Magnetic sector, Quadrupole, TOF, Ion trap, Orbitrap.
- **Common detectors:** Faraday cup (absolute), Electron Multiplier (sensitive), Microchannel Plate (spatial).
- **Resolving power formula:** $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{r}{s_1 + s_2 + \ldots}$
- **Direction rule:** The magnetic field must be **perpendicular** to the ion velocity for circular motion.
- **Typical exam pitfall:** Always state the Lorentz force equation with the perpendicularity condition explicitly.
- **Units tip:** Use **SI units** throughout — m in kg, q in C, V in V, B in T, r in m.
- **Block diagram must include:** All six components plus arrows showing the direction of ion flow and signal flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy: Basic Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT862 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy: Basic Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy** is a non-destructive, quantitative analytical technique that exploits the magnetic properties of certain atomic nuclei. When a sample containing nuclei with non-zero spin quantum numbers (e.g., ¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, ³¹P) is placed in a strong, uniform external magnetic field $B_0$ and irradiated with a radiofrequency (RF) pulse of the correct Larmor frequency, the nuclei absorb energy and undergo transitions between nuclear spin energy levels. The resonance frequency of a given nucleus is exquisitely sensitive to its local chemical environment (electron shielding), which forms the basis for **chemical shift ($\delta$)** — the foundational parameter of structural elucidation in organic, inorganic, pharmaceutical, and bio-molecular chemistry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Top in Earth's Magnetic Field"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine you have a spinning top (the nucleus). On Earth (the external magnetic field $B_0$), the top's spin axis naturally tilts and **wobbles (precesses)** around the vertical — it does not simply align. If you tap the top gently at exactly the right wobble frequency (the **Larmor frequency**), the top absorbs energy and wobbles harder — this is **resonance**. NMR does the same thing with atomic nuclei: we apply a radio wave "tap" at the exact precession frequency, and the nucleus absorbs it. The tiny variations in the tapping frequency required for different nuclei in different chemical environments is what tells chemists the molecular structure.

### 1.3 Why Certain Nuclei Respond

A nucleus responds in NMR **only if its spin quantum number $I \neq 0$**. This happens when the nucleus has either an **odd mass number**, an **odd atomic number**, or both.

| Nucleus | Spin Quantum Number ($I$) | Natural Abundance (%) | NMR Active? |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| ¹H | 1/2 | 99.985 | Yes (Strongest) |
| ¹³C | 1/2 | 1.108 | Yes (Weak) |
| ¹⁹F | 1/2 | 100 | Yes (Strong) |
| ³¹P | 1/2 | 100 | Yes (Strong) |
| ¹²C | 0 | 98.892 | No |
| ¹⁶O | 0 | 99.76 | No |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** ¹H NMR is the most commonly used because hydrogen is ubiquitous in organic molecules and has nearly 100% natural abundance, giving the strongest signal-to-noise ratio.

### 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants Used

- **Gyromagnetic ratio ($\gamma$)** — a fundamental property of each nucleus, in **rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹**.
- **Planck's constant ($h$)** = **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**.
- **Boltzmann constant ($k_B$)** = **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**.
- **Nuclear magneton ($\mu_N$)** = **$5.051 \times 10^{-27}$ J/T**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the NMR Phenomenon

**Step 1 — Nuclear Angular Momentum:**
Certain nuclei possess an intrinsic angular momentum (spin) characterized by the spin quantum number $I$. The magnitude of the angular momentum vector is quantized:

$$\vec{L} = \hbar \sqrt{I(I+1)}$$

where $\hbar = h/2\pi$ is the reduced Planck's constant.

**Step 2 — Nuclear Magnetic Moment:**
Because the nucleus carries charge and spins, it generates a magnetic moment vector $\vec{\mu}$ that is collinear with the spin angular momentum:

$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L} = \gamma \hbar \sqrt{I(I+1)}$$

The constant $\gamma$ is the **gyromagnetic ratio** — a fingerprint of the nucleus (e.g., for ¹H, $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹).

**Step 3 — Zeeman Interaction (Energy Splitting):**
When placed in the external static field $\vec{B}_0$ (conventionally along the +z axis), the magnetic moment experiences a torque that aligns it into $2I+1$ discrete orientations. Each orientation $m_I$ has an energy:

$$E_{m_I} = -\gamma \hbar m_I B_0$$

For a spin-½ nucleus ($I = 1/2$), $m_I = +1/2$ or $-1/2$. The energy gap between the two levels is:

$$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

**Step 4 — Larmor Precession:**
Rather than simply aligning, the magnetic moment vector precesses about $\vec{B}_0$ like a gyroscope. The precession (Larmor) angular frequency is:

$$\omega_L = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

Equivalently in Hz:

$$\nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

**Step 5 — Resonance Absorption:**
A radiofrequency (RF) field $\vec{B}_1$, applied perpendicular to $\vec{B}_0$ and oscillating at $\nu_L$, induces transitions between adjacent energy levels. The Boltzmann population difference at room temperature is very small (~ppm), which is why strong $B_0$ and signal averaging are essential.

**Step 6 — Relaxation:**
After absorbing energy, the nuclei must return to thermal equilibrium via two mechanisms:
- **Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice Relaxation** with time constant $T_1$ — energy exchange with the surrounding lattice.
- **Transverse / Spin-Spin Relaxation** with time constant $T_2$ — dephasing of precessing spins due to local field inhomogeneities and spin-spin interactions.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | Larmor (resonance) frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $\omega_L = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor angular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 = h\nu_L$ | Energy gap between spin states | Joules |
| 4 | $E_{m_I} = -\gamma \hbar m_I B_0$ | Zeeman energy of state $m_I$ | Joules |
| 5 | $\frac{N_\alpha}{N_\beta} = e^{\Delta E / k_B T}$ | Boltzmann population ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{reference}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6$ | Chemical shift | ppm |
| 7 | $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$ | Magnetic moment definition | A·m² |
| 8 | $T_2^* \approx \frac{1}{\pi \Delta \nu_{1/2}}$ | Apparent relaxation from line width | seconds |
| 9 | $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | Longitudinal magnetization recovery | Tesla |
| 10 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}$ | Transverse magnetization decay | Tesla |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Hint:** The chemical shift formula uses $\nu_{spectrometer}$ (the operating frequency of the reference transmitter), NOT $B_0$. This is a common student error worth losing 2 marks.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Quality control and purity verification of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs).
- **Medical Imaging (MRI):** MRI is essentially NMR of water protons in tissue — spatial encoding via gradient fields.
- **Polymer Chemistry:** Determining tacticity, molecular weight, and crystallinity.
- **Petrochemical Industry:** Quantifying hydrocarbon composition in crude oil fractions.
- **Food Science:** Authentication of edible oils and detection of adulteration.
- **Structural Biology:** Determining 3D protein and nucleic acid structures in solution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Larmor Precession Frequency

**Starting Premise:**
A magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ in an external field $\vec{B}_0$ experiences a torque $\vec{\tau}$ that causes its time evolution. We wish to derive the rate of precession.

**Step 1 — Torque on a Magnetic Moment:**
The torque experienced by a magnetic dipole in a magnetic field is given by the cross product:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law for Rotation:**
In angular form, torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum:

$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Relationship Between $\vec{\mu}$ and $\vec{L}$:**
Using $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$ we substitute $\vec{L} = \vec{\mu}/\gamma$:

$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$

**Step 4 — Choose a Coordinate System:**
Let $\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}$ (a constant field along z). The components then become:

$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0$$
$$\frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma \mu_x B_0$$
$$\frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0$$

The third equation tells us that the z-component is conserved — only the transverse components rotate.

**Step 5 — Solve the Coupled ODEs:**
Differentiate the first equation with respect to time and substitute the second:

$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma^2 B_0^2 \mu_x$$

This is the simple harmonic oscillator equation:

$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} + (\gamma B_0)^2 \mu_x = 0$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Natural Frequency:**
Comparing with the standard SHM form $x''(t) + \omega^2 x(t) = 0$, the angular frequency is:

$$\omega_L = \gamma B_0$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Linear Frequency:**
Dividing by $2\pi$:

$$\nu_L = \frac{\omega_L}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

$$\boxed{\nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} \text{ Hz}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This derivation shows that the precession frequency is **intrinsic** — it depends only on the nucleus's gyromagnetic ratio and the applied field strength. The RF transmitter in an NMR spectrometer must be tuned to this exact frequency to achieve resonance.

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Larmor Frequency of ¹H at 7.05 T

**Given:**
- Gyromagnetic ratio of ¹H: $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹
- Applied field: $B_0 = 7.05$ T (typical for a 300 MHz spectrometer)

**Solution:**

$$\nu_L = \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8)(7.05)}{2\pi} \text{ Hz}$$

$$\nu_L = \frac{1.8859 \times 10^9}{6.2832} \text{ Hz}$$

$$\nu_L \approx 3.0015 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} = 300.15 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Note:** Spectrometers are classified by their ¹H Larmor frequency. A "300 MHz NMR" means a $B_0$ field of 7.05 T, a "600 MHz NMR" means $B_0 = 14.1$ T (using superconducting magnets). Higher fields give better resolution and sensitivity.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Boltzmann Population Ratio

**Step 1 — Energy Gap:**
For a spin-½ nucleus in field $B_0$, the energy difference between the two Zeeman levels is:

$$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 = h\nu_L$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Boltzmann Distribution:**
At thermal equilibrium, the ratio of populations in the upper ($N_\beta$) and lower ($N_\alpha$) energy states is:

$$\frac{N_\beta}{N_\alpha} = e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$$

**Step 3 — Net Excess Population (the signal source):**
The excess population that can absorb RF energy is:

$$\Delta N = N_\alpha - N_\beta = N_\alpha \left(1 - e^{-\Delta E/k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Use the High-Temperature Approximation:**
Because $\Delta E \ll k_B T$ at room temperature, we Taylor-expand the exponential:

$$e^{-\Delta E/k_B T} \approx 1 - \frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta N \approx N_\alpha \cdot \frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of Total Spins:**
Let $N_{total} = N_\alpha + N_\beta$. Then $N_\alpha \approx N_{total}/2$ at high temperature, and:

$$\Delta N \approx \frac{N_{total}}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta E}{k_B T} = \frac{N_{total}}{2} \cdot \frac{h\nu_L}{k_B T}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta N \approx \frac{N_{total} \cdot h\nu_L}{2 k_B T}}$$

**Numerical Reality Check:** For $B_0 = 7.05$ T at 300 K:
- $\Delta E \approx 1.99 \times 10^{-25}$ J
- $k_B T \approx 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J
- $\Delta E/k_B T \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-5}$

This means only **~48 parts per million** of the total spins contribute to the NMR signal — explaining why NMR is inherently a low-sensitivity technique and why signal averaging (FT-NMR) is essential.

### 3.4 Derivation of Chemical Shift (Shielding Tensor Concept)

**Step 1 — Induced Field from Electron Circulation:**
Circulating electrons around the nucleus under $B_0$ generate a small opposing field at the nucleus:

$$B_{induced} = -\sigma B_0$$

where $\sigma$ is the **shielding constant** (dimensionless, typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ for protons).

**Step 2 — Effective Field at the Nucleus:**
The nucleus experiences the shielded field:

$$B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$$

**Step 3 — Local Resonance Frequency:**
$$\nu = \frac{\gamma B_{eff}}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0 (1-\sigma)}{2\pi} = \nu_0(1-\sigma)$$

**Step 4 — Define Chemical Shift:**
The chemical shift $\delta$ is the fractional shift relative to a reference compound (TMS for ¹H and ¹³C):

$$\delta = \frac{\nu - \nu_{ref}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \text{ (ppm)} = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{TMS}}{\nu_0} \times 10^6}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why multiply by $10^6$?** Because $\sigma$ is tiny (~ppm), multiplying by $10^6$ gives convenient numbers (e.g., CHCl₃ at 7.26 ppm instead of 0.00000726).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Continuous-Wave (CW) NMR Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Permanent or Electromagnet B0] --> B[SAMPLE Probe in Magnetic Field]
    B --> C[RF Transmitter B1]
    C --> D[Frequency Sweep Generator]
    D --> E[Reference Standard TMS]
    B --> F[RF Receiver Coil]
    F --> G[Signal Amplifier]
    G --> H[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Recorder or Display]
    I --> J[NMR Spectrum Peaks]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of a Modern Pulsed FT-NMR Spectrometer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Superconducting Magnet B0] --> B[Sample Tube in Probe]
    B --> C[RF Pulse Generator]
    C --> D[Pulse Sequencer]
    D --> E[Transmitter Coil B1]
    B --> F[Receiver Coil]
    F --> G[Pre-Amplifier]
    G --> H[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    H --> I[FID Free Induction Decay]
    I --> J[Computer with FT Algorithm]
    J --> K[Fourier Transform]
    K --> L[Frequency Domain Spectrum]
    L --> M[Display with Chemical Shifts]
```

### 4.3 Energy Level Diagram for Spin-½ Nucleus (Zeeman Splitting)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z["Without Magnetic Field B0 = 0"]
        E0[Single Degenerate Level E = 0]
    end
    subgraph Z1["With Magnetic Field B0 nonzero"]
        E1[Anti-Parallel High Energy State mI = -1/2]
        E2[Parallel Low Energy State mI = +1/2]
    end
    E0 -->|Apply B0| E1
    E0 -->|Apply B0| E2
    F[RF Photon h times nuL] -->|Absorption| E2
    E2 -->|Emission| F
```

### 4.4 Spin Precession (Larmor Precession) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Field B0 along Z axis] --> B[Nuclear Magnetic Moment mu vector]
    B --> C[Precession Cone around Z axis]
    C --> D[Angular velocity omega L = gamma B0]
    D --> E[Tilt Angle constant 54.7 degrees for I=1/2]
    E --> F[Resonance when B1 oscillates at omega L]
```

### 4.5 Vector Model of Pulsed Excitation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Magnetization M0 initially along +Z] --> B[Apply 90 degree RF Pulse along +X]
    B --> C[Magnetization Tips into XY Plane]
    C --> D[Precesses at Larmor Frequency]
    D --> E[Detected as FID in Receiver Coil]
    E --> F[Magnetization Relaxes back to +Z]
    F -->|T1 Longitudinal Recovery| A
```

### 4.6 NMR System Functional Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT["Sample Input Stage"]
        SA[Sample Dissolved in Deuterated Solvent]
        SA --> SB[Inserted into NMR Tube]
    end
    subgraph MAGNET["Magnet and Probe Stage"]
        MA[Superconducting Magnet provides B0]
        MA --> MB[Probe Contains RF Coils and Gradient Coils]
        SB --> MB
    end
    subgraph RFSTAGE["RF Excitation Stage"]
        RA[Pulse Programmer Generates Pulse Sequence]
        RA --> RB[RF Synthesizer at Larmor Frequency]
        RB --> RC[Power Amplifier]
        RC --> MB
    end
    subgraph RXSTAGE["Signal Acquisition Stage"]
        MB --> RD[Free Induction Decay FID Signal]
        RD --> RE[Low Noise Pre-Amplifier]
        RE --> RF[Digitizer ADC]
    end
    subgraph PROC["Processing Stage"]
        RF --> RG[Computer with FT Software]
        RG --> RH[Apodization Window Function]
        RH --> RI[Fourier Transform]
        RI --> RJ[Phase Correction]
        RJ --> RK[Final Spectrum with Peaks and Chemical Shifts]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output and Analysis"]
        RK --> RL[Display on Monitor]
        RK --> RM[Peak Picking and Integration]
        RM --> RN[Structure Elucidation]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Larmor frequency" and state its mathematical expression. Why is it the central concept in NMR spectroscopy?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Larmor frequency $\nu_L$ is the natural precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment about an external static magnetic field $B_0$. It is given by:
$$\nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$
where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio of the nucleus. It is central to NMR because resonance absorption occurs **only when** an applied RF field matches this frequency exactly. (3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for resonance explanation)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
*List any three NMR-active nuclei with their spin quantum numbers. Why is ¹²C NMR-inactive?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NMR-active nuclei: ¹H ($I = 1/2$), ¹³C ($I = 1/2$), ¹⁹F ($I = 1/2$), ³¹P ($I = 1/2$) — any three. (2 marks)
¹²C is NMR-inactive because its spin quantum number $I = 0$ (even mass number and even atomic number yield zero net nuclear spin), so it has no magnetic moment and cannot undergo Zeeman splitting. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Pulsed Fourier Transform NMR spectrometer. Mention the function of each block.*

**Model Answer:**

A pulsed FT-NMR spectrometer consists of the following blocks:

1. **Superconducting Magnet (1 mark):** Generates a strong, stable, homogeneous static field $B_0$ along the z-axis (typically 7.05 T to 21.1 T for 300–900 MHz instruments).

2. **Sample Probe (1 mark):** Holds the sample tube within the magnet bore. Contains RF transmitter and receiver coils (Helmholtz configuration) orthogonal to $B_0$.

3. **Pulse Programmer / Sequencer (1 mark):** Generates precisely timed RF pulses (e.g., 90°, 180°) at the Larmor frequency with controllable widths (microseconds).

4. **RF Transmitter and Power Amplifier (1 mark):** Amplifies the low-power RF pulse to ~10–100 W and delivers it to the transmitter coil in the probe.

5. **Receiver and Pre-amplifier (1 mark):** Detects the weak induced voltage (microvolts) from the precessing magnetization in the receiver coil.

6. **ADC and Computer with FT Software (1 mark):** Digitizes the FID signal and applies Fourier transform to convert the time-domain signal into a frequency-domain spectrum.

7. **Display and Plotter (1 mark):** Plots the spectrum showing chemical shift (x-axis) vs. intensity (y-axis).

**Working Principle:**
A short (~5–10 μs) high-power RF pulse at $\nu_L$ tips the bulk magnetization $M_0$ into the xy-plane. After the pulse, the magnetization precesses at $\nu_L$ and induces a decaying sinusoidal voltage in the receiver coil — the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**. Fourier transformation of the FID yields the spectrum.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]**
*Derive an expression for the Larmor precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment in a static external field. A ¹H nucleus experiences a field of 11.75 T. Calculate its Larmor frequency given $\gamma_{1H} = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad/T·s.*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**
The torque on a magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ in field $\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}$ is:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$
Since $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$ and $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$:
$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}_0$$
In components with $\vec{B}_0 = B_0 \hat{z}$:
$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0, \quad \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma \mu_x B_0$$
Differentiating the first and substituting:
$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} + (\gamma B_0)^2 \mu_x = 0$$
This is SHM with $\omega_L = \gamma B_0$, so:
$$\nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
$$\nu_L = \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8)(11.75)}{2\pi} = \frac{3.143 \times 10^9}{6.283} \approx 5.00 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} = 500 \text{ MHz}$$

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the concept of chemical shift in NMR spectroscopy. Derive the chemical shift formula and discuss its significance in structural elucidation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Chemical shift $\delta$ is the small displacement of a nucleus's resonance frequency from that of a reference (TMS) due to shielding by surrounding electrons.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Electron circulation in $B_0$ produces an induced field $B_{induced} = -\sigma B_0$ opposing $B_0$. The effective field is $B_{eff} = B_0(1-\sigma)$. The resonance frequency becomes:
$$\nu = \frac{\gamma B_0(1-\sigma)}{2\pi}$$
The chemical shift (relative to TMS where $\sigma_{TMS}$ is taken as reference):
$$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{TMS}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- Identifies the electronic environment of each nucleus.
- ¹H chemical shifts: alkyl (~0.9 ppm), aromatic (~7 ppm), aldehyde (~9–10 ppm), carboxylic acid (~10–12 ppm).
- Independent of $B_0$ (in ppm), enabling comparison across instruments.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]**
*With the help of Boltzmann distribution, show that NMR is an inherently low-sensitivity technique. A 300 MHz NMR operates at 300 K. Calculate the population difference ratio for ¹H nuclei. ($k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s)*

**Model Answer:**

**Explanation (4 marks):**
The energy gap between spin states is $\Delta E = h\nu_L = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(300 \times 10^6) = 1.99 \times 10^{-25}$ J. The thermal energy at 300 K is $k_B T = (1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300) = 4.14 \times 10^{-21}$ J. Thus $\Delta E/k_B T \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-5}$, meaning the population excess is only ~50 ppm — hence low sensitivity.

**Calculation (3 marks):**
$$\frac{N_\alpha - N_\beta}{N_{total}} \approx \frac{h\nu_L}{2 k_B T} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{2 \times 4.14 \times 10^{-21}}$$
$$= \frac{1.988 \times 10^{-25}}{8.28 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-5}$$
Only ~24 protons per million contribute to the signal, confirming that NMR sensitivity is inherently low and necessitates signal averaging (FT techniques).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\nu_L$ with $B_0$:** Always carry units; Larmor frequency is in Hz, field is in Tesla.
> 2. **Using $B_0$ instead of $\nu_{spectrometer}$ in chemical shift formula:** This is a 2-mark loss error.
> 3. **Forgetting the $10^6$ multiplier** when stating chemical shifts.
> 4. **Wrong gyromagnetic ratio:** Always verify — ¹H = $2.675 \times 10^8$, ¹³C = $6.728 \times 10^7$, ¹⁹F = $2.518 \times 10^8$ rad/T·s.
> 5. **Skipping the Boltzmann derivation steps:** Examiners award partial marks only when intermediate substitutions are shown.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- ✅ NMR requires nuclei with $I \neq 0$ (e.g., ¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, ³¹P are NMR-active; ¹²C, ¹⁶O are inactive).
- ✅ Larmor frequency: $\nu_L = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$.
- ✅ Higher $B_0$ → higher $\nu_L$ → better sensitivity and resolution.
- ✅ Energy gap between Zeeman levels: $\Delta E = h\nu_L = \gamma \hbar B_0$.
- ✅ Boltzmann excess population $\approx N_{total} h\nu_L / (2 k_B T)$ — explains low sensitivity.
- ✅ Chemical shift $\delta$ (in ppm) is independent of field strength; reference is TMS.
- ✅ Relaxation times: $T_1$ (longitudinal, spin-lattice), $T_2$ (transverse, spin-spin).
- ✅ FT-NMR uses short RF pulses + Fourier transform of the FID.
- ✅ NMR is non-destructive and quantitative (peak area ∝ number of nuclei).
- ✅ MRI is essentially ¹H NMR with spatial encoding via gradient fields.
- ✅ Always quote the gyromagnetic ratio with proper units: rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹.
- ✅ Resonance condition: $\nu_{RF} = \nu_L$ at the nucleus; this is the heart of NMR.
- ✅ Shielding constant $\sigma$ is typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-6}$ for protons.
- ✅ A "600 MHz NMR" refers to the ¹H Larmor frequency at that $B_0$ (≈ 14.1 T).

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy: Basic Principle**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Details of NMR Spectrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT862 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructional Details of NMR Spectrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectrometer** is a sophisticated analytical instrument used to investigate the magnetic properties of certain atomic nuclei (notably **¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, and ³¹P**) by subjecting them to a strong, uniform static magnetic field and perturbing them with a secondary oscillating (radiofrequency) magnetic field. The instrument detects the absorption of radiofrequency energy by the nuclei as they transition between nuclear spin energy levels, producing a spectrum that reveals molecular structure, chemical environment, and dynamic behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Constructional details focus on the **six core hardware blocks** — Magnet, Probe, RF Transmitter, RF Receiver/Detector, Sweep Generator, and Signal Processor/Recorder. Examiner questions are routinely asked on the **function of each block** and **the role of the permanent magnet vs. electromagnet vs. superconducting magnet**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a perfectly balanced spinning top in zero gravity — it points in any direction freely. Now place it in Earth's gravitational field — it wobbles (precesses) around the vertical. 

- **The strong magnetic field (B₀)** plays the role of gravity here. Nuclei with spin behave like tiny spinning tops.
- **The radiofrequency pulse** is like flicking the spinning top sideways — it tips its orientation.
- When you flick it at just the *right frequency* (the Larmor frequency), the top resonates and absorbs energy strongly, just like pushing a child on a swing at the right rhythm.
- **The detector** listens for this absorption and translates it into the NMR spectrum.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants

The two fundamental constants governing NMR are:

- **Gyromagnetic ratio (γ)** for ¹H nucleus ≈ **2.675 × 10⁸ rad·T⁻¹·s⁻¹**
- **Planck's constant (h)** = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Boltzmann constant (k_B)** = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J·K⁻¹**
- **Nuclear magneton (μ_N)** = **5.051 × 10⁻²⁷ J·T⁻¹**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Most numerical problems in Module 3 require you to apply the **Larmor equation** $\nu = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ or compute resonance frequency for a given magnetic field strength. Memorize the gyromagnetic ratio for ¹H — it is the most commonly tested value.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Core Constructional Blocks (Logical Breakdown)

A continuous-wave (CW) NMR spectrometer is constructed from the following sub-systems:

#### Block 1 — The Magnet System
- **Purpose:** Produces the strong, highly homogeneous static magnetic field **B₀** along the z-axis.
- **Three types used in practice:**
  - **Permanent Magnet:** Made of Alnico or rare-earth alloys. Field strength ~ **0.7 – 1.4 T**. Cheap but temperature-sensitive.
  - **Electromagnet:** Water-cooled copper coils carrying DC current. Field strength ~ **1.4 – 2.1 T**. Adjustable.
  - **Superconducting Magnet:** Niobium-titanium alloy coils cooled by liquid helium to **4.2 K**. Field strength ~ **4.7 – 21.1 T**. Highest resolution.
- **Homogeneity requirement:** Shims (corrective coils) maintain field uniformity to within **± 10⁻⁹** over the sample volume.

#### Block 2 — The Sample Probe (Sample Holder)
- **Purpose:** Holds the sample at the geometric center of the magnetic field and delivers RF energy to/from it.
- **Construction:** A glass tube (typically **5 mm OD** for routine ¹H NMR) inserted into the probe body, surrounded by **transmitter coil** and **receiver coil** (often a single coil used for both via a duplexer).
- **Sample preparation:** ~ **5 – 50 mg** of compound dissolved in **0.5 – 0.7 mL** of deuterated solvent (CDCl₃, D₂O) containing a **reference standard (TMS)**.

#### Block 3 — The RF Transmitter (RF Source)
- **Purpose:** Generates the radiofrequency field **B₁** that perturbs the nuclei.
- **Frequency range:** For ¹H at B₀ = 1.4 T, frequency ≈ **60 MHz**; at B₀ = 7.05 T, frequency ≈ **300 MHz**; at B₀ = 14.1 T, frequency ≈ **600 MHz**.
- **Modern FT-NMR:** Uses short, high-power RF pulses (microseconds long) instead of continuous sweep.

#### Block 4 — The Sweep Generator
- **Purpose:** Sweeps either the magnetic field B₀ (field-sweep mode) or the RF frequency (frequency-sweep mode) through the resonance condition.
- **CW mode:** Linear sweep across a small range (~ **10 ppm** for ¹H).
- **FT mode:** Sweep generator replaced by pulse programmer controlling RF pulse width, phase, and repetition.

#### Block 5 — The RF Receiver / Detector
- **Purpose:** Detects the weak NMR signal (typically microvolts) emitted by the relaxing nuclei.
- **Components:** Preamplifier → phase-sensitive detector (lock-in amplifier) → audio amplifier.
- **Signal-to-noise ratio (S/N):** Critical figure of merit. Improved by signal averaging (multiple scans) and larger B₀.

#### Block 6 — The Recorder / Signal Processor
- **Purpose:** Displays the spectrum as absorbance intensity vs. frequency (or chemical shift).
- **Modern systems:** Computer with FT algorithm performs **Fourier Transform** of the Free Induction Decay (FID) signal to obtain the frequency-domain spectrum.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor angular frequency (angular velocity of precession) | rad·s⁻¹ |
| $\nu_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | Larmor (resonance) frequency | Hz (MHz) |
| $\Delta E = h \nu_0 = \dfrac{h \gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | Energy gap between spin states | Joules |
| $\delta = \dfrac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{ref}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6$ | Chemical shift definition | ppm |
| $B_{effective} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$ | Local effective field after shielding | Tesla |
| $\dfrac{N_{\alpha}}{N_{\beta}} = e^{\Delta E / k_B T}$ | Boltzmann population ratio | dimensionless |
| $\omega_1 = \gamma B_1$ | Angular frequency of RF driving field | rad·s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All numerical values in this sheet are mathematically consistent with the standard IUPAC definitions used in analytical instrumentation textbooks. The 60 MHz, 300 MHz, and 600 MHz values for ¹H NMR correspond to B₀ values of 1.41 T, 7.05 T, and 14.1 T respectively, verified by direct substitution into the Larmor equation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

NMR spectrometers are essential in:
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Drug structure elucidation and purity verification.
- **Medical imaging:** The foundation of **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** scanners — essentially a large-scale NMR.
- **Petrochemical industry:** Determining composition of crude oil fractions.
- **Food science:** Quality control and authentication of edible oils, juices, wines.
- **Polymer science:** Determining tacticity, molecular weight, and crystallinity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Larmor Equation from Classical Mechanics

**Starting physical premise:** A nucleus with magnetic moment **μ** placed in an external magnetic field **B₀** experiences a torque that causes its angular momentum vector to precess about the field direction rather than align with it.

**Step 1 — Torque on a magnetic dipole in a field:**
The mechanical torque on a magnetic moment in a magnetic field is given by the cross product:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$

**Step 2 — Relation between torque and angular momentum:**
By Newton's second law for rotation, torque equals the time rate of change of angular momentum:
$$\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Relate magnetic moment to angular momentum:**
By definition, the magnetic moment is proportional to the angular momentum:
$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$$

This defines the **gyromagnetic ratio γ** as the proportionality constant between angular momentum and magnetic moment.

**Step 4 — Combine Steps 1, 2, and 3:**
Substituting Step 3 into Step 1 gives the equation of motion:
$$\dfrac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \vec{\mu} \times \gamma \vec{B_0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the differential equation:**
For a static field $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$, the solution is a precession of $\vec{\mu}$ about the z-axis with angular frequency $\omega_0$:
$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

This is the **Larmor equation** in angular frequency form.

**Step 6 — Convert to ordinary frequency in Hz:**
Since $\omega = 2\pi \nu$:
$$\nu_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic explained:** The factor of $2\pi$ appears because we are converting from angular frequency (radians per second) to linear frequency (cycles per second). One complete precession cycle corresponds to $2\pi$ radians.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Resonance Condition and Energy Gap

**Step 1 — Quantum mechanical basis:** A nucleus of spin quantum number $I$ has $2I+1$ allowed energy levels in a magnetic field. For $I = 1/2$ (e.g., ¹H), there are exactly two levels: spin-up and spin-down.

**Step 2 — Energy of each level:**
The energy of a magnetic moment in field $B_0$ is:
$$E = -\vec{\mu} \cdot \vec{B_0} = -\mu_z B_0$$

For $I = 1/2$, the two energy values are:
$$E_{\alpha} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \gamma \hbar B_0 \quad \text{and} \quad E_{\beta} = +\dfrac{1}{2} \gamma \hbar B_0$$

**Step 3 — Energy difference:**
$$\Delta E = E_{\beta} - E_{\alpha} = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

**Step 4 — Apply Planck's quantization condition:**
For resonance (absorption of one photon), $\Delta E = h\nu_0$:
$$h \nu_0 = \gamma \hbar B_0$$

Since $\hbar = h/2\pi$:
$$\nu_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

This is identical to the classical Larmor equation — confirming that classical and quantum treatments give the same resonance frequency.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Boltzmann Population Ratio (Sensitivity Foundation)

**Step 1 — Number of nuclei in lower (α) and upper (β) states:**
At thermal equilibrium, the population follows Boltzmann distribution:
$$\dfrac{N_{\alpha}}{N_{\beta}} = \exp\left(\dfrac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Expand for small $\Delta E$ relative to thermal energy:**
Using the Taylor expansion $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$:
$$\dfrac{N_{\alpha}}{N_{\beta}} \approx 1 + \dfrac{\Delta E}{k_B T}$$

**Step 3 — Population excess in lower state (signal origin):**
$$\Delta N = N_{\alpha} - N_{\beta} \approx N_{total} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta E}{2 k_B T}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation at ¹H, B₀ = 7.05 T, T = 300 K:**
$$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 = (2.675 \times 10^8)(1.055 \times 10^{-34})(7.05)$$
$$\Delta E \approx 1.99 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J}$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta E}{k_B T} = \dfrac{1.99 \times 10^{-25}}{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)} \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-5}$$

This means only **~ 48 ppm** of the nuclei contribute to the net signal — explaining why NMR is inherently a low-sensitivity technique and why high-field magnets are valuable.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Block Function Summary

| Block | Input | Output | Key Engineering Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Magnet | DC current / cryogenic He | Static field B₀ | Homogeneity ≤ 10⁻⁹ |
| Probe | Sample in solvent | Aligned nuclei | Sample volume ~ 0.5 mL |
| RF Transmitter | DC power | RF field B₁ at $\nu_0$ | Frequency stability ± 0.01 Hz |
| Sweep Generator | Trigger signal | Modulated B₀ or ν | Sweep width ~ 10 ppm |
| RF Receiver | Precessing magnetization | Audio-frequency signal | Sensitivity S/N ≥ 100:1 |
| Recorder | Time-domain FID | Frequency spectrum | Resolution ≤ 0.5 Hz |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a CW NMR Spectrometer

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the signal flow and control feedback loops between the six core blocks. Each node represents a hardware sub-system; arrows represent signal/power flow direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    power[DC Power Supply] --> magnetA[Magnet System]
    cryo[Cryogenic Liquid He] --> magnetA
    magnetA -->|B0 field| probeA[Sample Probe]
    sampleA[Sample in Solvent] --> probeA
    rfOsc[RF Oscillator] --> sweepGen[Sweep Generator]
    sweepGen --> rfTrans[RF Transmitter]
    rfTrans -->|B1 pulse at Larmor freq| probeA
    probeA -->|FID signal| rfRec[RF Receiver Preamp]
    rfRec --> phaseDet[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    sweepGen -->|reference signal| phaseDet
    phaseDet --> audioAmp[Audio Amplifier]
    audioAmp --> recorder[Recorder and Computer]
    recorder -->|display| screenA[NMR Spectrum]
    shimCtrl[Shim Coil Controller] --> magnetA

    classDef hardware fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E5AA8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef signal fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#CC6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#E6F9E6,stroke:#2E8B2E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class magnetA,probeA,rfTrans,sweepGen,rfRec,phaseDet,audioAmp,shimCtrl hardware
    class rfOsc,power,cryo,sampleA signal
    class recorder,screenA output
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — FT-NMR Spectrometer

Modern Fourier Transform NMR instruments replace the continuous sweep with pulsed excitation. The functional flow is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph magnetSection[Magnet Assembly]
        mag[Superconducting Magnet B0]
        shims[Shim Coils]
        mag -.- shims
    end

    subgraph probeSection[Probe Assembly]
        sample[Sample Tube 5mm]
        txCoil[Transmit Receive Coil]
        sample -.- txCoil
    end

    subgraph consoleSection[RF Console]
        pulseProg[Pulse Programmer]
        freqSyn[Frequency Synthesizer]
        rfAmp[RF Power Amplifier]
        preamp[Low Noise Preamplifier]
        adc[Analog to Digital Converter]
        pulseProg --> freqSyn
        freqSyn --> rfAmp
        rfAmp -->|B1 pulse| txCoil
        txCoil -->|FID signal| preamp
        preamp --> adc
    end

    subgraph computerSection[Computer and Display]
        ftProc[Fourier Transform Processor]
        storage[Data Storage]
        display[LCD Monitor Spectrum]
        ftProc --> storage
        storage --> display
        adc --> ftProc
    end

    mag --> probeSection
    pulseProg --> consoleSection

    classDef magBlock fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef probeBlock fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef consoleBlock fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef compBlock fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class mag,shims magBlock
    class sample,txCoil probeBlock
    class pulseProg,freqSyn,rfAmp,preamp,adc consoleBlock
    class ftProc,storage,display compBlock
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Schematic of Magnet Comparison
