The average of $t_{R1} + t_{R2}$ is:

$$t_{R1} + t_{R2} = t_M(1 + k_1') + t_M(1 + k_2') = 2 t_M \left(1 + \frac{k_1' + k_2'}{2}\right)$$

For closely spaced peaks ($k_1' \approx k_2' = k'$), the average becomes $2 t_M (1 + k_2')$:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k_1' (\alpha - 1)}{1 + k_2'}$$

### Step 5 — Final Purnell Form

Using $k_1' = k_2'/\alpha$:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k_2'}{1 + k_2'}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** When a KTU question asks "**How can resolution be improved?**", the above derivation gives the framework. The student should:
> 1. State the Purnell equation.
> 2. Explain each of the three factors.
> 3. State that $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$ — so doubling $N$ increases $R_s$ by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
> 4. Therefore, to double resolution by efficiency alone, you must quadruple $N$ (i.e., quadruple the column length).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimum Linear Velocity from van Deemter Equation

The **van Deemter equation**:

$$H(u) = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

To find the **minimum** of $H$ as a function of $u$:

$$\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0$$

Solving for $u$:

$$C = \frac{B}{u^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u^2 = \frac{B}{C}$$

$$\boxed{u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}}$$

At this velocity, the minimum plate height is:

$$H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{B \cdot C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** In GC, hydrogen and helium are preferred over nitrogen as carrier gases because they have **higher optimum velocities** and **broader flat regions** in the van Deemter plot, meaning the column remains efficient over a wider range of flow rates.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A component in a GC column has a retention time $t_R = 8.0$ min, a dead time $t_M = 1.0$ min, and a peak width at the base $W = 0.5$ min. Calculate:
> (a) the capacity factor $k'$
> (b) the number of theoretical plates $N$
> (c) the plate height $H$ if the column is $2.0$ m long.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Capacity Factor:**

$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{8.0 - 1.0}{1.0} = 7.0$$

**Part (b) — Number of Theoretical Plates:**

$$N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{W}\right)^2 = 16 \left(\frac{8.0}{0.5}\right)^2 = 16 \times 256 = 4096$$

**Part (c) — Plate Height:**

$$H = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{2.0 \text{ m}}{4096} = 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.488 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Verification:** A plate height of ~0.5 mm is typical for a well-packed GC column. The capacity factor $k' = 7$ is in the ideal range ($1 < k' < 10$).

---

## 3.4 The Distribution Constant and Retention — The Hidden Link

A more fundamental derivation links $t_R$ to the distribution constant $K$:

The fraction of time a molecule spends in the mobile phase is $\dfrac{1}{1 + k'}$, and in the stationary phase is $\dfrac{k'}{1 + k'}$.

If $V_M$ is the mobile phase volume and $V_S$ is the stationary phase volume:

$$k' = K \cdot \frac{V_S}{V_M}$$

Therefore:

$$t_R = t_M (1 + k') = t_M \left(1 + K \cdot \frac{V_S}{V_M}\right)$$

This shows that **retention time is directly proportional to the distribution constant $K$** and to the **volume ratio $V_S/V_M$** of stationary to mobile phase.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gas Chromatography — Complete Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **full functional architecture** of a Gas Chromatograph, including all major subsystems and signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CG1: Carrier Gas Cylinder] --> B[CG2: Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[CG3: Flow Controller]
    C --> D[CG4: Sample Injector]
    D --> E[CG5: Column in Temperature Controlled Oven]
    E --> F[CG6: Detector]
    F --> G[CG7: Amplifier]
    G --> H[CG8: Recorder or Data Station]
    H --> I[CG9: Chromatogram Output]
    D -->|Microsyringe| E
    F -->|Reference Signal| G
    H -->|Digital Storage| I

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style D fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style E fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style I fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

**Subsystem Color Legend:**
- **Blue** — Gas handling and supply subsystem.
- **Orange** — Injection and separation subsystem.
- **Purple** — Detection subsystem.
- **Green** — Data acquisition and display subsystem.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram with Signal Annotations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INJ[INJ: Sample Injection Port] -->|Vaporized Sample + Carrier Gas| COL[COL: Chromatographic Column]
    OVEN[OVEN: Column Oven with TC] -.->|Isothermal or Programmed Temperature| COL
    COL -->|Eluted Components| DET[DET: Detector TCD FID ECD]
    DET -->|Electrical Signal mV| AMP[AMP: Electrometer Amplifier]
    AMP -->|Processed Signal| REC[REC: Strip Chart Recorder or PC Based Integrator]
    REC -->|Chromatogram Peaks| OUT[OUT: Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis]

    style INJ fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style COL fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style OVEN fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style DET fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
    style AMP fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
    style REC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style OUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic: Inside the Column Oven

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GAS[CARRIER GAS INLET] --> REG[PRESSURE REGULATOR]
    REG --> INJ[INJECTION PORT HEATED BLOCK]
    INJ -->|Sample Vapor| SPLIT[Split or Splitless Vent]
    SPLIT --> COLP[PACKED OR CAPILLARY COLUMN COILED]
    COLP -->|Analyte Bands| DET[DETECTOR CELL]
    DET --> VENT[VENT OUTLET]
    OVENC[OVEN CHAMBER INSULATED] -.->|Air Circulation Fan| COLP
    HTR[OVEN HEATER ELEMENT] -.-> OVENC
    TCTR[TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER RTD] -.->|Feedback Signal| OVENC
    TCTR -.->|Setpoint Control| HTR

    style GAS fill:#bbdefb
    style REG fill:#bbdefb
    style INJ fill:#ffe0b2
    style COLP fill:#fff9c4
    style DET fill:#f8bbd0
    style OVENC fill:#eeeeee
    style HTR fill:#ffcdd2
    style TCTR fill:#c8e6c9
```

---

## 4.4 Constructional Details of Gas Chromatograph (Tabular Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most exam-relevant** for KTU 14-mark questions on GC construction.

| # | Component | Constructional Details |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Carrier Gas Supply** | High-purity gas from cylinder; usually **Helium**, **Hydrogen**, or **Nitrogen**. Two-stage pressure regulator reduces cylinder pressure (~2000 psi) to inlet pressure (10–50 psi). |
| **2** | **Flow Controller** | Maintains constant flow rate (typically 25–50 mL/min for packed columns; 1–30 mL/min for capillary). Uses a **soap bubble flow meter** for verification. |
| **3** | **Sample Injector** | Heated block (150–250°C) for vaporization. **Packed column injector** uses a microsyringe through a silicone septum. **Capillary column injector** uses **split** or **splitless** mode. **On-column** and **PTV (Programmable Temperature Vaporizer)** are advanced variants. |
| **4** | **Column** | Two types: (a) **Packed** — glass or stainless steel, 1–3 m long, 2–4 mm i.d., filled with stationary phase-coated solid support. (b) **Capillary (Open Tubular)** — fused silica, 15–100 m long, 0.1–0.5 mm i.d., stationary phase coated on inner wall (WCOT) or on a porous support layer (SCOT). |
| **5** | **Column Oven** | Insulated chamber with a forced-air fan for uniform temperature. Supports **isothermal** (constant T) and **temperature programming** (T increases over time, e.g., 50→300°C at 10°C/min). Range: 4–450°C. |
| **6** | **Detector** | Senses eluting components. Common types: **TCD** (Thermal Conductivity Detector), **FID** (Flame Ionization Detector), **ECD** (Electron Capture Detector), **FPD** (Flame Photometric Detector), **MS** (Mass Spectrometer). |
| **7** | **Recorder / Data System** | Analog **strip-chart recorder** or modern **PC-based integrator / chromatography data system (CDS)** with peak area integration. |

---

## 4.5 Detailed Constructional Cross-Section of a Capillary Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OUTER: Polyimide Coating] --> B[MIDDLE: Fused Silica Tubing]
    B --> C[INNER: Stationary Phase Film 0.1 to 5 micrometer]
    C --> D[SAMPLE: Analyte Molecules Partitioning]

    style A fill:#cfd8dc
    style B fill:#90a4ae
    style C fill:#ffcc80
    style D fill:#a5d6a7
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **thickness of the stationary phase film** directly affects column efficiency: thinner films give **lower mass transfer resistance (smaller C term)** and thus **higher efficiency**, but with **lower sample capacity**.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Chromatography and Explain its Classification.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Chromatography is an analytical technique for separating the components of a mixture based on the **differential distribution** of the components between two immiscible phases: a **stationary phase** and a **mobile phase**.

**Classification of Chromatography:**

**(a) Based on the shape of the chromatographic bed:**
1. **Planar Chromatography** — e.g., Paper Chromatography, Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC).
2. **Column Chromatography** — e.g., Packed Column, Capillary Column.

**(b) Based on the physical state of the mobile phase:**
1. **Gas Chromatography (GC)** — mobile phase is gas; includes GLC and GSC.
2. **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** — mobile phase is liquid; includes LSC, LLC, HPLC, Ion-Exchange, Size-Exclusion.

**(c) Based on the separation mechanism:**
1. Adsorption Chromatography
2. Partition Chromatography
3. Ion-Exchange Chromatography
4. Size-Exclusion Chromatography
5. Affinity Chromatography

---

### **Question 2: Define the Following Terms: (i) Retention Time, (ii) Capacity Factor, (iii) Selectivity Factor.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Retention Time ($t_R$):**
The total time taken by a component to travel from the point of injection to the detector, measured up to the **peak maximum** of that component in the chromatogram.

**(ii) Capacity Factor ($k'$):**
The ratio of the **adjusted retention time** of a component to the **dead time** of the column. It indicates how strongly a component is retained by the stationary phase.

$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{t_R'}{t_M}$$

**(iii) Selectivity Factor ($\alpha$):**
The ratio of the capacity factors of two adjacent peaks (always taken as the **larger** value divided by the **smaller**).

$$\alpha = \frac{k_2'}{k_1'} \quad (k_2' > k_1')$$
A value $\alpha > 1$ indicates the two components can be separated.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **two alternative questions (A and B)** are provided. The student answers **either** A **or** B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Draw and Explain the Block Diagram of a Gas Chromatograph. List the Functions of Each Block. [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram of a Gas Chromatograph is shown below:

```
┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
│ Carrier  │───▶│ Pressure │───▶│  Flow    │───▶│ Sample   │
│Gas Cyl.  │    │Regulator │    │Controller│    │Injector  │
└──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └────┬─────┘
                                                      │
                                                      ▼
┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
│ Recorder │◀───│Amplifier │◀───│ Detector │◀───│ Column in│
│   / CDS  │    │          │    │          │    │  Oven    │
└──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘
```

**Functions of Each Block:**

1. **Carrier Gas Cylinder:** Supplies the mobile phase gas (He, H₂, or N₂) at high pressure. Must be of high purity to avoid detector noise and column contamination.

2. **Pressure Regulator:** Reduces the high cylinder pressure (~2000 psi) to a stable inlet pressure (10–50 psi) suitable for the column.

3. **Flow Controller:** Maintains a **constant mass flow rate** of carrier gas through the column. Critical for reproducible retention times.

4. **Sample Injector:** A heated port (150–250°C) where the liquid sample is vaporized by a microsyringe through a self-sealing silicone septum and swept into the column by the carrier gas. In capillary GC, split/splitless modes are used.

5. **Column + Oven:** The **heart** of the GC. The stationary phase (a high-boiling liquid or solid) is packed or coated inside the column. Components partition between the stationary and mobile phases and elute at different times. The oven (4–450°C) provides isothermal or programmed temperature control.

6. **Detector:** Converts the presence of each eluting component into an electrical signal. Examples:
   - **TCD** — measures changes in thermal conductivity.
   - **FID** — measures ion current from a hydrogen flame.
   - **ECD** — measures current reduction due to electron capture.
   - **MS** — mass-to-charge based detection.

7. **Amplifier:** Boosts the weak detector signal (often microvolts to millivolts) to a level suitable for recording.

8. **Recorder / CDS (Chromatography Data System):** Plots detector signal vs. time, producing the **chromatogram**. Modern systems integrate peak areas for quantitative analysis.

#### **(b) Explain in Detail the Constructional Details of a GC Column. [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

A GC column is the **separation chamber** and is available in two main types:

**Type 1: Packed Column**

- **Material:** Borosilicate glass or stainless steel.
- **Dimensions:** Length **1–3 m**, internal diameter **2–4 mm**, shaped as a coil.
- **Packing:** Solid support (e.g., **Chromosorb P**, diatomaceous earth, 80–100 mesh) is coated with a **liquid stationary phase** (e.g., OV-101 silicone oil) at 1–10% by weight.
- **Advantages:** Robust, larger sample capacity, lower cost.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower efficiency (fewer theoretical plates).

**Type 2: Capillary (Open Tubular) Column**

- **Material:** **Fused silica** (SiO₂) tube drawn to a fine capillary.
- **Dimensions:** Length **15–100 m**, internal diameter **0.1–0.5 mm** (commonly 0.25 mm).
- **Outer coating:** Polyimide protective layer for flexibility.
- **Stationary phase:** Coated on the inner wall in three sub-types:
  - **WCOT (Wall-Coated Open Tubular):** Liquid phase directly on inner wall; film thickness 0.1–5 μm.
  - **SCOT (Support-Coated Open Tubular):** Liquid phase on a thin layer of solid support on the wall.
  - **PLOT (Porous-Layer Open Tubular):** Solid adsorbent (e.g., alumina) on the inner wall.
- **Advantages:** Extremely high efficiency (N up to 10⁶ plates), excellent resolution, fast analysis.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower sample capacity, requires more sensitive detectors.

**Other Constructional Features:**

- The column is housed in a **thermostatted oven** with a fan for uniform temperature.
- **Temperature programming** allows the oven temperature to be ramped (e.g., 50°C → 300°C at 10°C/min), enabling separation of analytes with a wide boiling-point range.
- Column ends are connected via **graphite ferrules** to the injector and detector, ensuring leak-tight seals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse **HETP** with **H** — they are the same quantity but examiners expect consistent notation.
> - Do **NOT** say "the column separates components" without explaining **how** — always mention **differential partitioning** between stationary and mobile phases.
> - Many students forget to mention the **column oven** and its role — this is a common mark-loss area.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) With a Neat Block Diagram, Explain the Principle and Working of Gas Chromatography. [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Gas Chromatography:**

Gas Chromatography is based on the principle of **differential partitioning** of the components of a vaporized sample between a **mobile gas phase** (carrier gas) and a **stationary phase** (a high-boiling liquid or an active solid).

When a mixture is injected into the carrier gas stream, each component distributes itself between the two phases according to its **distribution constant** $K$:

$$K = \frac{C_s}{C_m}$$

Components with **high $K$** (more soluble / more strongly adsorbed in the stationary phase) elute **later**, while those with **low $K$** elute **earlier**. This time-based separation produces a **chromatogram** with distinct peaks.

**Working (Block Diagram):**

1. The **carrier gas** (He/H₂/N₂) flows from the cylinder through a pressure regulator and flow controller.
2. The **sample** (typically 0.1–10 μL liquid or gas) is injected via a microsyringe into the heated injection port, where it is **vaporized**.
3. The vapor is swept by the carrier gas into the **column** housed in a temperature-controlled oven.
4. Inside the column, components **partition** between the mobile gas phase and the stationary liquid/solid phase, separating into bands.
5. The **eluted components** enter the **detector**, producing a signal proportional to their concentration.
6. The signal is **amplified** and recorded as a function of time, producing the **chromatogram**.

**Qualitative Analysis:** Based on **retention time** ($t_R$).
**Quantitative Analysis:** Based on **peak area** or **peak height** (compared to standards).

#### **(b) Explain the Constructional Details and Working of TCD and FID Detectors. [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**A. Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD):**

**Construction:**
- A **Wheatstone bridge** with four filament arms (typically tungsten or rhenium-tungsten).
- Two filaments are exposed to **pure carrier gas** (reference side), and two are exposed to the **column eluent** (sample side).
- Filaments are housed in a temperature-controlled metal block.

**Working:**
- The carrier gas cools the filaments by thermal conduction. Each filament is heated by a constant current.
- When a sample component elutes, the **thermal conductivity of the gas mixture decreases** (since the analyte has lower thermal conductivity than He or H₂).
- This causes the **filament temperature to rise**, changing its resistance, and **unbalancing the bridge**.
- The imbalance produces a **voltage signal** proportional to the analyte concentration.

**Characteristics:** Universal, non-destructive, lower sensitivity, suitable for gas analysis.

**B. Flame Ionization Detector (FID):**

**Construction:**
- A **hydrogen/air flame** burns at a small jet.
- Above the flame is a **collector electrode** (anode) and the jet acts as the cathode.
- An **electrometer amplifier** measures the tiny ion current (~10⁻¹² A).

**Working:**
- The column eluent is mixed with **H₂** and **air** and burned.
- Carbon-containing compounds are **ionized** in the flame, producing ions and electrons:

$$CH + O \rightarrow CHO^+ + e^-$$

- The **ion current** flows between the electrodes and is measured by the electrometer.
- The current is **proportional to the number of carbon atoms** burned, hence to the analyte mass.

**Characteristics:** Destructive, high sensitivity, no response to permanent gases (CO₂, H₂O, CO), most popular for organic analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - In TCD, students often forget to mention the **Wheatstone bridge configuration** — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> - In FID, students often confuse the **carrier gas** (N₂ or He) with the **detector gases** (H₂ and air) — they are different. Be precise.
> - Always mention whether the detector is **destructive** or **non-destructive** — this is frequently asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Bullet Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute Revision)**

### Classification & Terminology
- ✔ **Chromatography** = differential distribution between **stationary** and **mobile** phases.
- ✔ **Planar** vs. **Column** — based on bed shape.
- ✔ **GC** vs. **LC** — based on physical state of mobile phase.
- ✔ **Adsorption / Partition / Ion-Exchange / Size-Exclusion / Affinity** — based on mechanism.
- ✔ **Retention time** ($t_R$): time from injection to peak maximum.
- ✔ **Dead time** ($t_M$): time for non-retained component.
- ✔ **Adjusted retention time** $t_R' = t_R - t_M$.
- ✔ **Capacity factor** $k' = t_R'/t_M$ — ideal range $1 < k' < 10$.
- ✔ **Selectivity factor** $\alpha = k_2'/k_1'$ — must be $> 1$ for separation.
- ✔ **Resolution** $R_s$ — must be $\geq 1.5$ for baseline separation.
- ✔ **Number of plates** $N = 16(t_R/W)^2$.
- ✔ **HETP** $H = L/N$.

### Resolution Master Equation
- ✔ $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k_2'}{1 + k_2'}$
- ✔ $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$ — quadrupling $N$ doubles $R_s$.

### van Deemter Equation
- ✔ $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$
- ✔ $A$ = Eddy diffusion (minimized by uniform small particles).
- ✔ $B/u$ = Longitudinal diffusion (minimized by higher $u$).
- ✔ $C \cdot u$ = Mass transfer resistance (minimized by thin films).
- ✔ $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$.

### Gas Chromatography — Essentials
- ✔ **Carrier gases:** He, H₂, N₂ (H₂ and He preferred — broader van Deemter minimum).
- ✔ **Column types:** Packed (1–3 m, 2–4 mm i.d.) and Capillary (15–100 m, 0.1–0.5 mm i.d.).
- ✔ **Stationary phase:** High-boiling liquid (silicone oil) for GLC; solid adsorbent for GSC.
- ✔ **Detectors:** TCD (universal, non-destructive), FID (sensitive to organics, destructive), ECD (halogen-selective), FPD (S and P selective), MS (mass-based).
- ✔ **Temperature programming:** Increases oven temperature over time to elute high-boiling components.

### Common KTU 3-Mark Question Triggers
- ✔ "Define chromatography and classify it."
- ✔ "Define capacity factor / selectivity factor / resolution."
- ✔ "Write the van Deemter equation and explain each term."
- ✔ "List the components of GC."

### Common KTU 14-Mark Question Triggers
- ✔ "Explain the principle, construction, and working of GC with block diagram."
- ✔ "Derive the Purnell resolution equation."
- ✔ "Explain TCD and FID in detail."
- ✔ "Explain the constructional details of a packed vs. capillary column."

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Details, GC Detectors, Liquid Chromatography: Types of Liquid Chromatography, High Pressure Liquid Chromatography (HPLC): Principle, Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT862 - ANALYTICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Column Details, GC Detectors, Liquid Chromatography: Types of Liquid Chromatography, High Pressure Liquid Chromatography (HPLC): Principle, Constructional Details ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Gas Chromatography (GC) — Column Details **Formal Definition:** A **Gas Chromatography (GC) column** is the core separation unit of a GC system where the analyte mixture (in vapor phase) is partitioned between a **mobile phase** (carrier gas, e.g., Helium, Nitrogen, Hydrogen) and a **stationary phase** (high-boiling liquid film coated on an inert solid support or on the inner wall of a capillary). Separation occurs based on differential partitioning coefficients ($K$) of components between the two phases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Columns are classified based on (i) **Packing geometry** — *Packed columns* vs *Capillary (Open Tubular) columns*, and (ii) **Stationary phase polarity** — *Non-polar*, *Intermediate polarity*, and *Polar* columns. **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a long, narrow corridor filled with sticky honey on the walls. People (analyte molecules) running through (carrier gas flow) at the same speed will stick to the walls for different durations depending on how "sticky" they find the honey. Lighter/non-interactive molecules exit first; heavier/sticky molecules exit last. The GC column does exactly this — but at the molecular level! #### 1.1.1 Packed Columns | Feature | Specification | | :--- | :--- | | Geometry | Stainless steel / glass tube, **1–5 m** long, **2–4 mm** internal diameter | | Stationary Phase | Liquid (5–10%) coated on **Chromosorb P, W, G** solid support (60–80 mesh) | | Carrier Gas Flow | **25–50 mL/min** | | Sample Size | Large (micrograms) | | Theoretical Plates | ~**3,000–10,000** | #### 1.1.2 Capillary (Open Tubular) Columns | Feature | Specification | | :--- | :--- | | Geometry | Fused silica tube, **10–100 m** long, **0.1–0.53 mm** internal diameter | | Types | **WCOT** (Wall Coated), **SCOT** (Support Coated), **PLOT** (Porous Layer) | | Stationary Phase Film | **0.1–5 µm** thick (cross-linked polysiloxanes) | | Carrier Gas Flow | **1–25 mL/min** | | Theoretical Plates | Up to **150,000+** | > [!NOTE] > **Van Deemter (1956)** demonstrated that capillary columns offer superior efficiency because the **Eddy Diffusion (A-term)** is eliminated due to the open tubular path. --- ### 1.2 GC Detectors — The Sensing Heart **Formal Definition:** A **GC Detector** is a device that continuously monitors the **effluent stream** from the column and transduces a physical/chemical property change of the carrier gas + analyte into a measurable **electrical signal** (typically current or voltage), which is plotted as the chromatogram. **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the GC column as a marathon race. The detector is the **photo-finish camera** at the end — it doesn't care *how* the runners ran, only *when* and *how strongly* each one crossed the line. The detector's job is to register each compound's arrival with high sensitivity and minimal distortion. #### 1.2.1 Universal vs Selective Detectors > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Detector** → Responds to **all** compounds (e.g., TCD). > **Selective Detector** → Responds only to **specific** class of compounds (e.g., ECD for halogens, FID for hydrocarbons). #### 1.2.2 Major GC Detector Types | Detector | Full Form | Principle | Detection Limit | Selectivity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **TCD** | Thermal Conductivity | Measures change in thermal conductivity of gas | **10⁻⁶ g** | Universal | | **FID** | Flame Ionization | Measures ions produced in H₂/air flame | **10⁻¹² g** | Hydrocarbons | | **ECD** | Electron Capture | Measures ionization current change | **10⁻¹³ g** | Halogens, nitro | | **FPD** | Flame Photometric | Measures optical emission (S, P) | **10⁻¹¹ g** | S, P compounds | | **MS** | Mass Spectrometry | Measures mass-to-charge ratio | **10⁻¹² g** | Universal + ID | | **PID** | Photo Ionization | UV lamp ionizes analyte | **10⁻¹¹ g** | Aromatics | --- ### 1.3 Liquid Chromatography (LC) — Types **Formal Definition:** **Liquid Chromatography (LC)** is a separation technique in which the **mobile phase is a liquid** (solvent or solvent mixture) and the stationary phase is either a **solid (adsorbent)** or a **liquid film on a solid support**. Components are separated based on differences in **adsorption**, **partition**, **ion-exchange**, **size**, or **affinity**. **Intuitive Analogy:** LC is like a **toll-booth system on a highway** where different vehicles (molecules) stop for different durations based on their "pass type" (affinity for stationary phase). Some zip through (non-retained), others take longer (retained), creating a natural time-based separation. #### 1.3.1 Classification of LC Techniques | Type | Stationary Phase | Separation Basis | Mobile Phase Force | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Adsorption (LSC)** | Silica, Alumina | Polarity / Adsorption | Gravity / Pump | | **Partition (LLC)** | Liquid on support | Partition coefficient (K) | Pump | | **Ion-Exchange** | Resin (cation/anion) | Charge | Pump | | **Size Exclusion (SEC/GPC)** | Porous gel | Molecular size | Pump | | **Affinity** | Bound ligand | Bio-specific interaction | Pump | | **HPLC** | Micro-particle bonded phase | Partition / Adsorption | High-pressure pump | > [!NOTE] > **Planar Chromatography** includes **TLC** (Thin Layer) and **Paper Chromatography** — used in KTU syllabus as preliminary qualitative tools. --- ### 1.4 HPLC — Principle & Constructional Details **Formal Definition:** **High Pressure Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)** is an advanced form of liquid chromatography in which the mobile phase (solvent) is forced under **high pressure (up to 6000 psi / ~400 bar)** through a column packed with **microparticulate stationary phase (3–10 µm)**. The high pressure ensures reasonable flow rates and sharp, efficient separations comparable to GC. **Intuitive Analogy:** HPLC is like an **express highway with toll plazas that have very narrow, precisely-engineered lanes**. The high pressure (highway speed) ensures every "vehicle" (molecule) moves through quickly, while the tightly-packed, micro-sized particles (narrow lanes) force each molecule to interact more thoroughly, producing razor-sharp separation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "High Pressure"?** > The small particle size (3–10 µm) creates enormous flow resistance. Hagen-Poiseuille's law shows flow rate $\propto d^{4}$ — so halving particle diameter increases back-pressure by **16×**. Hence pumps must deliver **1000–6000 psi**. --- ### 1.5 The Complete HPLC — System Snapshot **Definition of Components:** - **Solvent Reservoir** → Stores and degasses mobile phase - **Pump** → Delivers high-pressure, pulse-free flow - **Injector** → Introduces sample (5–50 µL) without depressurizing - **Column** → Heart of separation (C18, C8, Silica, etc.) - **Detector** → Identifies eluting compounds (UV-Vis, RI, ELSD, MS) - **Recorder / Data System** → Plots chromatogram ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of GC Column Efficiency #### 2.1.1 The Van Deemter Equation (1956) The **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP, H)** is governed by three band-broadening contributions: $$ H \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u $$ Where: - $A$ = **Eddy Diffusion** term (multiple flow paths) - $\frac{B}{u}$ = **Longitudinal Diffusion** term (molecular spreading) - $C \cdot u$ = **Mass Transfer** term (lag in stationary/mobile phase equilibrium) - $u$ = **Linear velocity of carrier gas** (cm/s) > [!NOTE] > **Optimal Linear Velocity $u_{opt}$** is obtained by differentiating $H$ with respect to $u$ and setting $\frac{dH}{du} = 0$, yielding: > > $$u_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}$$ #### 2.1.2 The Golay Equation (for Capillary Columns) Since capillary columns have **no packing**, the $A$-term = 0: $$ H \;=\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C_{m} \cdot u \;+\; C_{s} \cdot u $$ Where $C_m$ and $C_s$ are mobile-phase and stationary-phase mass transfer coefficients. #### 2.1.3 Number of Theoretical Plates ($N$) $$ N \;=\; 16 \left(\frac{t_{R}}{W}\right)^{2} \;=\; 5.54 \left(\frac{t_{R}}{W_{1/2}}\right)^{2} $$ Where $t_R$ = retention time, $W$ = peak width at baseline, $W_{1/2}$ = peak width at half-height. #### 2.1.4 Resolution Equation $$ R_{s} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'} $$ Where: - $\alpha$ = **Selectivity factor** = $k'_{2} / k'_{1}$ - $k'$ = **Capacity factor** = $\frac{t_{R} - t_{M}}{t_{M}}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** The famous **"Knox-Saleem" resolution equation** for HPLC is: > > $$R_{s} \;=\; \frac{1}{4} \sqrt{N} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'}$$ --- ### 2.2 GC Detector Performance Parameters #### 2.2.1 Detector Sensitivity ($S$) $$ S \;=\; \frac{I}{C \cdot Q} $$ Where: - $I$ = detector output current (A) - $C$ = analyte concentration in carrier gas (g/mL) - $Q$ = carrier gas flow rate (mL/s) #### 2.2.2 Minimum Detectable Quantity (MDQ) $$ MDQ \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot N}{S} $$ Where $N$ = noise level (A). #### 2.2.3 Linear Dynamic Range (LDR) $$ LDR \;=\; \frac{\text{Upper linear limit}}{\text{Minimum detectable quantity}} $$ --- ### 2.3 HPLC — Theoretical Foundations #### 2.3.1 Reduced Plate Height and Velocity (Knox Plot) For HPLC, the dimensionless **reduced parameters** are used: $$ h \;=\; \frac{H}{d_{p}} \qquad \nu \;=\; \frac{u \cdot d_{p}}{D_{m}} $$ The **Knox equation**: $$ h \;=\; A \cdot \nu^{0.33} \;+\; \frac{B}{\nu} \;+\; C \cdot \nu $$ > [!NOTE] > For "good" columns, $h < 3$ when $\nu \approx 3$–$5$. KTU students must know this dimensionless form. #### 2.3.2 Darcy's Law for Back-Pressure The pressure drop across a packed HPLC column is given by: $$ \Delta P \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot L \cdot u}{k_{0} \cdot d_{p}^{2}} $$ Where: - $\eta$ = mobile phase viscosity - $L$ = column length - $u$ = linear velocity - $k_{0}$ = specific permeability (constant) - $d_{p}$ = particle diameter > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world implication:** Reducing $d_p$ from 10 µm to 5 µm **quadruples** back-pressure. This is why ultra-high-pressure systems (UHPLC) use **2.7 µm core-shell particles** to maintain efficiency at standard pressures. --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Variables | Unit | Application | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$ | $A,B,C,u$ | cm, cm/s | Van Deemter (GC) | | 2 | $N = 16(t_R / W)^2$ | $t_R, W$ | time | Plate count | | 3 | $k' = (t_R - t_M) / t_M$ | $t_R, t_M$ | time | Capacity factor | | 4 | $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$ | $k'$ | dimensionless | Selectivity | | 5 | $R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha-1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1+k'}$ | $N, \alpha, k'$ | dimensionless | Resolution | | 6 | $H_{eff} = L / N_{eff}$ | $L, N_{eff}$ | cm | Effective plate height | | 7 | $\Delta P = \eta L u / (k_0 d_p^2)$ | $\eta, L, u, d_p$ | Pa | HPLC back-pressure | | 8 | $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$ | $B, C$ | cm/s | Optimal velocity | | 9 | $L = N \cdot H$ | $N, H$ | cm | Column length | | 10 | $h = H/d_p$, $\nu = u d_p / D_m$ | $H, d_p, u, D_m$ | dimensionless | Reduced parameters | > [!NOTE] > **Key Boundaries to Remember:** > - $k' < 1$ → peaks elute too fast, poor resolution > - $1 < k' < 10$ → ideal analytical range > - $k' > 30$ → excessive analysis time, broad peaks > - $\alpha = 1$ → **NO separation possible** regardless of $N$ ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Linear Velocity from Van Deemter Equation **Goal:** Derive $u_{opt}$ that minimizes $H$. **Step 1: Start with the Van Deemter equation** $$ H(u) \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u $$ **Step 2: Differentiate $H$ with respect to $u$** $$ \frac{dH}{du} \;=\; -\frac{B}{u^{2}} \;+\; C $$ **Step 3: Set derivative to zero for minimum** $$ -\frac{B}{u^{2}} \;+\; C \;=\; 0 $$ **Step 4: Rearrange to solve for $u$** $$ C \;=\; \frac{B}{u^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u^{2} \;=\; \frac{B}{C} $$ **Step 5: Take the positive root (velocity is positive)** $$ u_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} $$ **Step 6: Substitute back to get minimum plate height** $$ H_{min} \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{\sqrt{B/C}} \;+\; C \cdot \sqrt{B/C} $$ $$ H_{min} \;=\; A \;+\; 2\sqrt{B \cdot C} $$ > [!NOTE] > This minimum is a **convex minimum** since $\frac{d^{2}H}{du^{2}} = \frac{2B}{u^{3}} > 0$, confirming it is a true minimum. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship between Plate Count and Resolution **Goal:** Show how $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$. **Step 1: Start with the resolution definition** $$ R_{s} \;=\; \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{W_{1} + W_{2}} $$ **Step 2: Assume similar peak widths** ($W_1 \approx W_2 = W$) $$ R_{s} \;=\; \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{W} $$ **Step 3: Express $W$ in terms of plate count** From $N = 16(t_R / W)^2$, we get $W = 4 t_R / \sqrt{N}$. **Step 4: Substitute into resolution equation** $$ R_{s} \;=\; \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{4 t_R / \sqrt{N}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N} \cdot (t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{4 t_R} $$ **Step 5: Introduce $\alpha$ and $k'$** Since $t_R = t_M (1 + k')$ and $k'_2 = \alpha \cdot k'_1$: $$ R_{s} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1 + k'} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Insight:** To **double** $R_s$, plate count $N$ must increase by **4×**. Since $N = L/H$, this requires **quadrupling the column length** (or halving $H$ via smaller particles). --- ### 3.3 Numerical Example: HPLC Column Design **Problem:** Design an HPLC separation for two compounds with $\alpha = 1.10$, $k' = 5$, particle diameter $d_p = 5 \, \mu m$. Find the column length needed for $R_s = 1.5$. **Given:** - $\alpha = 1.10$ - $k' = 5$ - $d_p = 5 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, cm$ - Target $R_s = 1.5$ **Step 1: Calculate selectivity term** $$ \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \;=\; \frac{0.10}{1.10} \;=\; 0.0909 $$ **Step 2: Calculate retention term** $$ \frac{k'}{1 + k'} \;=\; \frac{5}{6} \;=\; 0.8333 $$ **Step 3: Rearrange resolution equation to solve for $N$** $$ N \;=\; \left( \frac{4 \cdot R_s \cdot \alpha}{\alpha - 1} \cdot \frac{1 + k'}{k'} \right)^{2} $$ $$ N \;=\; \left( \frac{4 \times 1.5 \times 1.10}{0.10} \times \frac{6}{5} \right)^{2} $$ $$ N \;=\; (66.0 \times 1.2)^{2} \;=\; (79.2)^{2} \;=\; 6273 $$ **Step 4: Assume reduced plate height $h = 3$ (typical for good column)** $$ H \;=\; h \cdot d_{p} \;=\; 3 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \, cm $$ **Step 5: Calculate required column length** $$ L \;=\; N \cdot H \;=\; 6273 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 9.4 \, cm $$ **Step 6: Engineering reality check** In practice, columns are **15 cm** long (commercial standard) to provide a **safety margin** and account for extra-column band broadening. > [!NOTE] > This is a typical KTU numerical — practice with $\alpha$, $k'$, $N$, $L$ conversions. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Detector Comparison for KTU | Detector | Min Detectable (g/s) | Linear Range | Destructive? | Carrier Gas Compatibility | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | TCD | $10^{-6}$ | $10^{4}$ | No | Universal | | FID | $10^{-12}$ | $10^{7}$ | Yes (flame burns analyte) | H₂, He, N₂ | | ECD | $10^{-13}$ | $10^{4}$ | No | N₂, Ar-CH₄ | | FPD | $10^{-11}$ | $10^{3}$ | Yes | H₂, He, N₂ | | PID | $10^{-11}$ | $10^{7}$ | Yes | N₂, Air | | MS | $10^{-12}$ | $10^{5}$ | Yes (ionization) | He preferred | > [!IMPORTANT] > **FID is the KTU favourite** because of its universal response to organics, exceptional sensitivity, wide linear range, and rugged construction. --- ### 3.5 Step-by-Step: FID Working **Step 1:** Effluent from GC column mixes with **H₂** and **air**. **Step 2:** Mixture burns at the **jet** (burner tip). **Step 3:** Hydrocarbon combustion produces ions and electrons: $\text{CH} + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{CHO}^{+} + e^{-}$ **Step 4:** Collector electrode (held at +300 V) collects the ions. **Step 5:** Current flows through high-impedance resistor → voltage signal. **Step 6:** Signal amplified → recorded as peak. **FID Response Equation:** $$ I \;=\; K \cdot (\text{rate of carbon atoms entering flame}) $$ > [!NOTE] > FID does **NOT** respond to: $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{CO}$, $\text{HCOOH}$, $\text{HCHO}$, inorganic gases. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete GC System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Carrier Gas

[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD) used in Gas Chromatography.* **Model Answer:** > A **Thermal Conductivity Detector (TCD)** is a universal, non-destructive GC detector that measures changes in the thermal conductivity of the carrier gas stream. **Construction:** - A metal block containing a **cavity** with a heated **filament** (tungsten or rhenium-tungsten) or **thermistor**. - Two such filaments form a **Wheatstone bridge** — one in the **reference** stream (pure carrier gas), one in the **sample** stream (column effluent). **Working:** 1. Carrier gas flows over both filaments. With pure carrier gas, both filaments lose heat at the **same rate** → bridge is **balanced** → output = 0. 2. When an analyte elutes from the column, it **changes the thermal conductivity** of the gas stream. 3. The sample filament **cools differently** (analytes usually have lower thermal conductivity than He/H₂). 4. Filament **resistance changes** → Wheatstone bridge **unbalances** → measurable **voltage signal** proportional to analyte concentration. **Key Equations:** $$ \Delta V \propto G \cdot (C_{2} - C_{1}) $$ Where $G$ = detector response factor, $C_1, C_2$ = thermal conductivities of carrier and carrier + analyte. **Advantages:** Universal, non-destructive, simple, works with any carrier gas. **Disadvantages:** Low sensitivity ($\sim 10^{-6}$ g), sensitive to flow and temperature fluctuations. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Compare Normal Phase and Reversed Phase HPLC with suitable examples.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | **Normal Phase HPLC** | **Reversed Phase HPLC** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Stationary Phase | **Polar** (Silica, -CN, -NH₂) | **Non-polar** (C18, C8, Phenyl) | | Mobile Phase | **Non-polar** (Hexane, CH₂Cl₂, IPA) | **Polar** (Water, MeOH, ACN, Buffer) | | Elution Order | **Polar compounds elute last** | **Polar compounds elute first** | | Retention Mechanism | Adsorption / Hydrogen bonding | Hydrophobic interaction | | Sample Polarity | Non-polar to moderately polar | Polar to moderately non-polar | | Typical Application | Isomers, lipids, fat-soluble vitamins | Pharmaceuticals, peptides, nucleotides | | Column Example | Silica (e.g., μPorasil) | ODS / C18 (e.g., μBondapak) | > The "**normal**" rule: polar stationary phase + non-polar mobile phase = "normal" because it mimics the natural polarity of silica. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. In HPLC block diagrams, students often forget to show the **degasser** and **guard column** — losing 2 marks. > 2. For Van Deemter, do not write the equation without defining each term — lose 1 mark. > 3. For detector questions, students confuse **selective** vs **specific** detectors. Selective = responds to a *class*; Specific = responds to *one compound only*. > 4. Always write the **column dimensions** (L × ID) when describing HPLC columns. > 5. In numerical problems, **always state units** in the final answer. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the **constructional details and working principle of HPLC** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **HPLC = High Performance Liquid Chromatography** (also called High Pressure LC). **Principle:** Separation is based on differential distribution of analyte components between a **liquid mobile phase** (forced at high pressure) and a **stationary phase** packed in a column. The high pressure (1000–6000 psi) is needed to overcome the resistance of microparticulate (3–10 µm) packing. **Block Diagram:** (Refer Section 4.2) **Component-wise Description:** | # | Component | Function | Specification | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Solvent Reservoir** | Stores mobile phase (often 2 reservoirs for gradient) | Glass, degassed by He sparging | | 2 | **Degasser** | Removes dissolved O₂, N₂ that cause bubble noise | Vacuum membrane type | | 3 | **Pump** | Delivers constant, pulse-free high-pressure flow | Reciprocating or syringe type | | 4 | **Mixer / Gradient Former** | Mixes solvents in programmed ratios | Low-dead-volume dynamic mixer | | 5 | **Injector** | Introduces sample (5–50 µL) into flowing stream | Rheodyne valve, 6-port, 20 µL loop | | 6 | **Guard Column** | Protects analytical column from particulates | 2–5 cm, same packing | | 7 | **Analytical Column** | Performs separation | 15–25 cm × 4.6 mm ID, 5 µm C18 | | 8 | **Thermostat** | Maintains constant column temperature | 25–60 °C | | 9 | **Detector** | Measures eluting compounds | UV-Vis (most common), RI, ELSD, MS | | 10 | **Data System** | Records chromatogram, integrates peaks | Computer + software | **Working Flow:** Mobile phase → degassed → pumped → mixed → sample injected via loop → flows through guard → analytical column → separated peaks → detector → signal → chromatogram. **Why High Pressure?** To achieve reasonable flow rates ($u \approx 1$ mm/s) through packed microparticle columns. Darcy's law shows $\Delta P \propto 1/d_p^2$. --- **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **principle and construction of Flame Ionization Detector (FID)**. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Principle:** When organic compounds are burned in a H₂-air flame, **ions and electrons** are produced. The resulting **ion current** is measured and is proportional to the mass of carbon entering the flame per unit time. **Construction (Refer Section 4.6):** - **Jet / Burner tip:** Stainless steel, where H₂ + carrier gas + sample burns. - **Collector electrode:** Platinum, positioned 5–10 mm above jet, polarized at **+300 V** (DC) via a polarizing electrode. - **Igniter:** Heated filament or spark ignites the flame. - **Gas supplies:** H₂ (30 mL/min), Air (300 mL/min), Carrier (He/N₂, 30 mL/min). - **Electrometer amplifier:** Converts picoampere current to millivolt signal. **Working Mechanism:** $$ \text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{m} \;+\; \text{O}_2 \; \xrightarrow{\text{flame}} \; \text{CHO}^{+} \;+\; e^{-} \;+\; \text{neutral products} $$ The $\text{CHO}^{+}$ ions are attracted to the **negatively polarized collector** (or the jet acts as anode). The tiny current ($\sim 10^{-12}$ A) flows through a **high-impedance resistor** ($10^{8}$–$10^{10}$ Ω), producing a measurable voltage: $$ V_{out} \;=\; I \times R \;=\; 10^{-12} \times 10^{10} \;=\; 10^{-2} \, V \;=\; 10 \, mV $$ **Performance:** - **Detection limit:** $\sim 10^{-12}$ g carbon/s - **Linear dynamic range:** $10^{7}$ - **Response:** Proportional to **number of carbon atoms** in molecule **Advantages:** - Excellent sensitivity and wide linear range - Universal for organics (responds to all C-containing compounds) - Rugged, reliable, reproducible - Non-responds to H₂O, CO₂, CO, inorganic gases — ideal for trace organics in aqueous samples **Limitations:** - **Destructive** — sample is burned - Requires H₂ and air supply (flammable!) - Does **not** respond to non-hydrocarbons (HCHO, HCOOH, CO₂, CO) - Cannot detect noble gases, H₂O, H₂S --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Van Deemter equation** and discuss how it influences the **selection of carrier gas velocity** in GC. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** The **Van Deemter equation** (1956) describes how the **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP, $H$)** of a chromatographic column varies as a function of the **linear velocity ($u$)** of the mobile phase: $$ H \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u $$ **Term-by-Term Analysis:** **(1) Eddy Diffusion Term ($A$):** - Caused by **multiple flow paths** through packed column. - Independent of flow rate. - $A = 2\lambda d_p$, where $\lambda$ = packing irregularity, $d_p$ = particle size. - **Reduced to zero** in capillary (open tubular) columns. **(2) Longitudinal Diffusion Term ($B/u$):** - Spontaneous molecular diffusion along the column axis. - $B = 2\gamma D_m$, where $\gamma$ = obstruction factor, $D_m$ = diffusion coefficient in mobile phase. - Dominates at **low velocities**. - Higher in **gaseous mobile phase** (GC) than liquid. **(3) Mass Transfer Term ($C \cdot u$):** - Lag in equilibration between mobile and stationary phase. - $C = C_s + C_m$: stationary phase + mobile phase contributions. - Dominates at **high velocities**. **Graphical Interpretation:** A plot of $H$ vs $u$ shows a **U-shaped curve** with minimum at: $$ u_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} \qquad H_{min} \;=\; A \;+\; 2\sqrt{BC} $$ **Influence on Carrier Gas Selection:** - **Hydrogen (H₂)** → Highest $D_m$ → high $B$ term → optimum at higher $u$ → faster analysis. - **Helium (He)** → Medium $D_m$ → safer, intermediate optimum. - **Nitrogen (N₂)** → Lowest $D_m$ → optimum at low $u$ → slow, but highest $N$. **KTU Engineering Tip:** Working at $u = u_{opt}$ gives **minimum $H$** (maximum efficiency). Working at $u = 2u_{opt}$ only increases $H$ by ~10% but doubles speed — a common compromise in **practical KTU problems**. --- **(b)** Discuss in detail the **classification and working of liquid chromatography** techniques. Compare HPLC with classical LC. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Classification of LC (Refer Section 4.4):** **(1) Adsorption Chromatography (LSC):** - Stationary phase: Polar solid (Silica, Alumina) - Separation: Differential adsorption of solutes on solid surface - Application: Separation of isomers, lipophilic compounds **(2) Partition Chromatography (LLC):** - Stationary phase: Liquid film on inert support - Separation: Differential solubility between two immiscible liquids - Application: Polar compounds (sugars, amino acids) **(3) Ion-Exchange Chromatography (IEC):** - Stationary phase: Charged resin (cation or anion exchanger) - Separation: Differential electrostatic attraction of charged species - Application: Amino acids, proteins, inorganic ions, water analysis **(4) Size Exclusion Chromatography (SEC / GPC):** - Stationary phase: Porous gel (Sephadex, polystyrene) - Separation: Molecular size — large molecules excluded first - Application: Polymer molecular weight distribution **(5) Affinity Chromatography:** - Stationary phase: Bound ligand (e.g., enzyme inhibitor, antibody) - Separation: Bio-specific lock-and-key binding - Application: Protein purification **Comparison Table: HPLC vs Classical LC** | Feature | Classical LC | **HPLC** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Particle Size | 50–150 µm | **3–10 µm** | | Column Length | 50–200 cm | **10–25 cm** | | Pressure | Gravity (low) | **1000–6000 psi** | | Analysis Time | Hours | **Minutes (5–30 min)** | | Plate Count | $\sim 100$–$500$ | **$\sim 5,000$–$15,000$** | | Resolution | Low | **High** | | Detection | Manual, visual | **Automated (UV, RI, etc.)** | | Sample Size | mg | **µg** | | Cost | Low | High | | Reproducibility | Variable | **Excellent** | **Advantages of HPLC:** - High resolution and efficiency - Short analysis time - Sensitive detection - Reproducible and automatable - Suitable for non-volatile and thermally labile compounds (unlike GC) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** **GC Columns:** - ✅ Packed columns: 1–5 m × 2–4 mm, 3,000–10,000 plates - ✅ Capillary columns: 10–100 m × 0.1–0.53 mm, up to 150,000 plates - ✅ WCOT / SCOT / PLOT classifications - ✅ Carrier gases: H₂ (fastest), He (compromise), N₂ (highest efficiency) **GC Detectors:** - ✅ TCD = Universal, non-destructive, low sensitivity - ✅ FID = Universal for organics, $10^{-12}$ g, destructive, doesn't respond to H₂O/CO₂ - ✅ ECD = Selective for halogens, $10^{-13}$ g, uses Ni-63 radioactive source - ✅ FPD = Selective for S and P (uses optical filter) - ✅ Sensitivity $S = I / (C \cdot Q)$; MDQ = $2N/S$ **LC Types:** - ✅ Adsorption, Partition, Ion-exchange, Size-exclusion, Affinity - ✅ Normal phase = polar stationary, non-polar mobile - ✅ Reversed phase = non-polar stationary (C18), polar mobile **HPLC:** - ✅ Pressure: 1000–6000 psi (modern UHPLC up to 1500 bar) - ✅ Particle size: 3–10 µm (UHPLC uses <2 µm core-shell) - ✅ Columns: 15–25 cm × 4.6 mm ID (standard analytical) - ✅ Components: Reservoir → Degasser → Pump → Mixer → Injector → Guard → Column → Detector → Recorder - ✅ Most common detector: UV-Vis (variable wavelength), then DAD (diode array) - ✅ Darcy's law: $\Delta P \propto 1/d_p^2$ **Key Equations:** - ✅ Van Deemter: $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ - ✅ Plate count: $N = 16(t_R/W)^2$ - ✅ Capacity factor: $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$ - ✅ Resolution: $R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha-1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'}{1+k'}$ - ✅ Optimal velocity: $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$ - ✅ Reduced parameters: $h = H/d_p$, $\nu = u d_p / D_m$ - ✅ HPLC back-pressure: $\Delta P = \eta L u / (k_0 d_p^2)$ **Golden Rules:** - ⭐ $\alpha = 1$ → No separation possible regardless of $N$ - ⭐ To double $R_s$, need 4× more plates or 2× column length - ⭐ $1 < k' < 10$ → ideal analytical range - ⭐ Smaller $d_p$ → higher $N$ but exponentially higher $\Delta P$ - ⭐ HPLC handles **non-volatile** and **thermally labile** compounds (vs GC) - ⭐ FID is the "workhorse" detector; TCD is the "universal" backup; ECD for environmental halogenated pesticides
